GV.OC-01.title: Mission-Informed Cybersecurity Risk Management
GV.OC-01.assessorFocus: Verify that the organization has documented its mission and that cybersecurity risk management decisions are explicitly linked to mission objectives.

evidenceItems.name: Mission and Vision Statement with Cybersecurity Linkage
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that organizational mission is formally documented and referenced in cybersecurity planning
whatGoodLooksLike: Mission statement explicitly references protection of critical assets and services
Cybersecurity strategy document traces objectives back to mission priorities
Risk assessment criteria incorporate mission impact as a scoring factor
commonGaps: Mission statement exists but cybersecurity strategy makes no reference to it
Cybersecurity risk decisions are made in isolation from business objectives
No documented linkage between mission-critical functions and security controls
suggestedSources: Strategic planning office or executive leadership
CISO or cybersecurity governance team

evidenceItems.name: Cybersecurity Risk Management Strategy Document
evidenceItems.description: Shows that risk management strategy is derived from and aligned with organizational mission
whatGoodLooksLike: Strategy document includes a section explicitly mapping mission objectives to cybersecurity goals
Annual review cycle ensures continued mission alignment
Strategy is approved by senior leadership with mission oversight authority
commonGaps: Strategy document is purely technical with no business context
No evidence of leadership review or approval
Strategy has not been updated to reflect mission changes
suggestedSources: Cybersecurity governance documentation repository
Board or executive committee meeting minutes

evidenceItems.name: Risk Assessment with Mission Impact Analysis
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that risk assessments consider impact to organizational mission
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk register entries include mission impact ratings
Risk scoring methodology weights mission criticality
Risk treatment decisions reference mission priorities
commonGaps: Risk assessments focus solely on technical severity without mission context
No standardized method for scoring mission impact
Mission-critical systems are not identified or prioritized in risk assessments
suggestedSources: Enterprise risk management team
Cybersecurity risk assessment reports

assessmentTips: Ask leadership to articulate how cybersecurity priorities reflect the organization's mission
Trace a recent risk decision back to mission objectives to verify alignment
Check whether mission changes (e.g., new service lines) triggered cybersecurity strategy updates
Review whether mission-critical systems receive proportionally higher security investment

GV.OC-02.title: Stakeholder Identification and Expectations
GV.OC-02.assessorFocus: Confirm that the organization has identified both internal and external stakeholders and that their cybersecurity expectations are documented, considered, and addressed in risk management activities.

evidenceItems.name: Stakeholder Register
evidenceItems.description: Documents internal and external stakeholders with their cybersecurity-related needs and expectations
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive list of internal stakeholders (board, executives, employees) with documented expectations
External stakeholders (customers, regulators, partners) identified with specific cybersecurity requirements
Register is reviewed and updated at least annually
commonGaps: Stakeholder identification is informal or undocumented
External stakeholders such as supply chain partners are omitted
Stakeholder expectations are not linked to cybersecurity controls or policies
suggestedSources: Governance, risk, and compliance (GRC) team
Legal and compliance department

evidenceItems.name: Stakeholder Communication Plan
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates how stakeholder expectations are communicated and integrated into cybersecurity risk management
whatGoodLooksLike: Defined communication channels and frequencies for different stakeholder groups
Evidence of feedback mechanisms for stakeholders to express cybersecurity concerns
Communication plan is aligned with risk reporting cadence
commonGaps: No formal communication plan exists for cybersecurity topics
Communication is ad hoc and reactive rather than planned
Key stakeholder groups are excluded from cybersecurity communications
suggestedSources: CISO office or cybersecurity governance function
Corporate communications or stakeholder relations team

evidenceItems.name: Stakeholder Requirements Traceability Matrix
evidenceItems.description: Shows how stakeholder expectations map to cybersecurity controls and risk treatment decisions
whatGoodLooksLike: Clear traceability from stakeholder needs to specific controls or policies
Conflicting stakeholder expectations are identified and resolved
Matrix is maintained as a living document with version control
commonGaps: No formal mapping between stakeholder expectations and cybersecurity measures
Matrix exists but is outdated or incomplete
Stakeholder expectations are acknowledged but not operationalized
suggestedSources: GRC platform or documentation repository
Risk management team

assessmentTips: Interview representatives from different stakeholder groups to validate that their expectations are accurately captured
Verify that stakeholder expectations influenced at least one recent risk decision
Check for evidence that customer and regulatory expectations are tracked separately and addressed

GV.OC-03.title: Legal, Regulatory, and Contractual Requirements
GV.OC-03.assessorFocus: Assess whether the organization maintains a current inventory of applicable legal, regulatory, and contractual cybersecurity obligations, and whether compliance is systematically tracked and managed.

evidenceItems.name: Regulatory and Legal Requirements Register
evidenceItems.description: Comprehensive inventory of applicable laws, regulations, and standards with cybersecurity implications
whatGoodLooksLike: Register covers all jurisdictions where the organization operates
Each requirement is mapped to responsible owners and applicable controls
Register is updated when new regulations are enacted or existing ones change
commonGaps: Register is incomplete, missing recently enacted regulations
No assigned ownership for tracking regulatory changes
Privacy regulations (GDPR, CCPA) are tracked by legal but not integrated with cybersecurity
suggestedSources: Legal and compliance department
GRC platform or compliance tracking system

evidenceItems.name: Contractual Cybersecurity Obligations Tracker
evidenceItems.description: Documents cybersecurity requirements from contracts with customers, partners, and suppliers
whatGoodLooksLike: All active contracts with cybersecurity clauses are cataloged
Contractual obligations are mapped to implementing controls
Compliance with contractual requirements is periodically verified
commonGaps: Contractual cybersecurity obligations are not centrally tracked
No process to extract and operationalize cybersecurity clauses from new contracts
Contract renewals do not trigger security requirement reviews
suggestedSources: Procurement or vendor management office
Legal department contract repository

evidenceItems.name: Compliance Assessment Reports
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of periodic assessments against legal, regulatory, and contractual requirements
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular compliance assessments are conducted and documented
Gaps are identified with remediation plans and timelines
Assessment results are reported to senior leadership
commonGaps: Compliance assessments are performed only when audited externally
No remediation tracking for identified compliance gaps
Assessments cover only a subset of applicable requirements
suggestedSources: Internal audit team
External audit or assessment reports

assessmentTips: Request the complete regulatory register and verify it includes recent legislative changes in all operating jurisdictions
Sample several contracts to confirm cybersecurity clauses are extracted and tracked
Ask how the organization learns about new regulatory requirements and how quickly they are incorporated

GV.OC-04.title: Critical Objectives and Services for Stakeholders
GV.OC-04.assessorFocus: Verify that the organization has identified its critical capabilities and services that external stakeholders depend on, established criticality criteria, and communicated resilience objectives.

evidenceItems.name: Business Impact Analysis (BIA)
evidenceItems.description: Documents critical business functions, their dependencies, and the impact of disruption
whatGoodLooksLike: BIA covers all business functions with defined criticality tiers
Recovery time objectives (RTOs) and recovery point objectives (RPOs) are established for critical services
BIA is reviewed and updated at least annually or after significant organizational changes
commonGaps: BIA is outdated and does not reflect current business operations
RTOs and RPOs are aspirational rather than tested and validated
BIA does not account for dependencies on external stakeholders or services
suggestedSources: Business continuity planning team
Operations or service delivery management

evidenceItems.name: Critical Services Catalog
evidenceItems.description: Inventory of services and capabilities that external stakeholders depend on, with criticality ratings
whatGoodLooksLike: All externally facing services are cataloged with criticality classifications
Stakeholder dependencies are explicitly documented for each service
Catalog is cross-referenced with BIA and risk assessments
commonGaps: No formal catalog of externally dependent services exists
Criticality is informally understood but not documented
Internal and external service dependencies are conflated
suggestedSources: Service management or IT operations team
Product or service delivery leadership

evidenceItems.name: Resilience Objectives Communication Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that resilience objectives for critical services are communicated to relevant stakeholders
whatGoodLooksLike: Resilience objectives are published in SLAs or service descriptions
Internal teams are aware of RTOs and RPOs for services they support
Communication records show periodic updates to stakeholders on resilience posture
commonGaps: Resilience objectives exist on paper but are not communicated to operational teams
SLAs do not include cybersecurity resilience commitments
No evidence that stakeholders are informed of changes to resilience objectives
suggestedSources: Service level management documentation
Stakeholder communications archive

assessmentTips: Ask operational staff to name the organization's most critical services and compare with the documented catalog
Verify that BIA findings directly inform cybersecurity resource allocation decisions
Check whether resilience objectives are tested through exercises or tabletop scenarios

GV.OC-05.title: External Dependencies and Services
GV.OC-05.assessorFocus: Confirm that the organization has identified external outcomes, capabilities, and services it depends on, documented potential failure points, and communicated this information to appropriate personnel.

evidenceItems.name: External Dependency Inventory
evidenceItems.description: Catalog of external resources, services, and providers the organization depends on
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive inventory including cloud providers, utilities, ISPs, and key suppliers
Each dependency is mapped to the organizational assets and functions it supports
Dependencies are classified by criticality and single-point-of-failure risk
commonGaps: Inventory is incomplete, missing shadow IT or informal external services
Dependencies are tracked in siloed departmental lists rather than centrally
No classification of dependencies by criticality or failure impact
suggestedSources: IT asset management or service catalog
Procurement and vendor management records

evidenceItems.name: Dependency Risk and Failure Analysis
evidenceItems.description: Analysis of external dependencies as potential points of failure for critical capabilities
whatGoodLooksLike: Each critical dependency has a documented failure scenario and impact assessment
Alternative or redundant options are identified for single points of failure
Analysis is updated when new dependencies are introduced or existing ones change
commonGaps: No formal analysis of dependency failure scenarios exists
Single points of failure are known informally but not documented or mitigated
Analysis does not consider cascading failures across dependent services
suggestedSources: Risk management or business continuity team
IT architecture or infrastructure team

evidenceItems.name: Dependency Communication and Sharing Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that dependency information is shared with appropriate personnel for operational awareness
whatGoodLooksLike: Dependency maps are accessible to incident response and continuity teams
Periodic briefings include updates on changes to critical external dependencies
Dependency information is integrated into incident response runbooks
commonGaps: Dependency information is maintained by one team but not shared broadly
Incident responders are unaware of critical external dependencies
No process to update teams when external dependencies change
suggestedSources: Incident response team documentation
Internal communication or briefing records

assessmentTips: Ask the incident response team to describe key external dependencies and compare with the documented inventory
Verify that dependency analysis includes cloud and SaaS services adopted by business units
Check whether dependency failures have been simulated in continuity exercises

GV.RM-01.title: Risk Management Objectives
GV.RM-01.assessorFocus: Verify that cybersecurity risk management objectives are formally established, measurable, agreed upon by organizational stakeholders, and used to guide risk decisions.

evidenceItems.name: Cybersecurity Risk Management Objectives Document
evidenceItems.description: Formal documentation of near-term and long-term cybersecurity risk management objectives
whatGoodLooksLike: Objectives are specific, measurable, and time-bound
Objectives are updated annually and after major organizational changes
Clear linkage between objectives and enterprise risk appetite
commonGaps: Objectives are vague or generic (e.g., 'improve security posture') without measurable targets
Objectives have not been updated in over two years
No formal agreement or sign-off from senior stakeholders
suggestedSources: CISO office or cybersecurity governance team
Strategic planning documentation

evidenceItems.name: Stakeholder Agreement Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that senior leaders and stakeholders have reviewed and agreed to risk management objectives
whatGoodLooksLike: Meeting minutes showing leadership discussion and endorsement of objectives
Formal sign-off or approval records from board or executive committee
Stakeholder feedback is documented and incorporated
commonGaps: Objectives are set by cybersecurity team without executive input or approval
No documented evidence of stakeholder review or agreement
Leadership engagement is limited to annual compliance sign-off without substantive discussion
suggestedSources: Board or executive committee meeting minutes
Governance records or approval workflows

evidenceItems.name: Risk Management Performance Metrics
evidenceItems.description: Metrics and KPIs used to measure progress toward cybersecurity risk management objectives
whatGoodLooksLike: Defined KPIs aligned to each risk management objective
Regular reporting cadence with trend analysis
Metrics inform decision-making on resource allocation and strategy adjustments
commonGaps: No metrics are defined for risk management objectives
Metrics are collected but not reported to leadership
Metrics are activity-based (e.g., patches applied) rather than outcome-based (e.g., risk reduction)
suggestedSources: Cybersecurity dashboard or reporting platform
GRC system reports

assessmentTips: Ask leadership to articulate the organization's cybersecurity risk management objectives in their own words
Verify that objectives are referenced in risk treatment decisions and resource allocation
Check whether objectives were updated after significant incidents or organizational changes

GV.RM-02.title: Risk Appetite and Tolerance Statements
GV.RM-02.assessorFocus: Confirm that the organization has established, documented, communicated, and periodically reviewed risk appetite and risk tolerance statements that guide cybersecurity decision-making.

evidenceItems.name: Risk Appetite Statement
evidenceItems.description: Board-approved statement defining the organization's overall willingness to accept cybersecurity risk
whatGoodLooksLike: Clear, qualitative statement approved by the board or senior leadership
Statement addresses different risk categories (operational, reputational, financial, compliance)
Statement is periodically reviewed and updated based on risk exposure changes
commonGaps: No formal risk appetite statement exists
Statement is too vague to guide operational decisions
Statement was created once and never revisited
suggestedSources: Board governance documentation
Enterprise risk management framework

evidenceItems.name: Risk Tolerance Thresholds and Criteria
evidenceItems.description: Specific, measurable thresholds that translate risk appetite into operational decision criteria
whatGoodLooksLike: Quantitative thresholds defined for key risk indicators (e.g., maximum acceptable downtime, data loss limits)
Tolerance levels are defined per risk category or asset classification
Escalation triggers are defined when tolerance thresholds are approached or exceeded
commonGaps: Risk appetite exists but is not translated into measurable tolerance levels
Tolerance thresholds are inconsistent across business units
No defined escalation process when tolerances are exceeded
suggestedSources: CISO office risk management documentation
Enterprise risk management team

evidenceItems.name: Risk Appetite Communication and Acknowledgment Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that risk appetite and tolerance statements are communicated to and understood by relevant personnel
whatGoodLooksLike: Communication records showing distribution to risk owners and decision-makers
Training or briefing materials that explain risk appetite in practical terms
Evidence that risk appetite statements are referenced in risk acceptance decisions
commonGaps: Risk appetite statements are confined to governance documents and not operationalized
Risk owners are unaware of organizational risk tolerance levels
Risk acceptance decisions do not reference established tolerance criteria
suggestedSources: Internal communications or training records
Risk acceptance decision documentation

assessmentTips: Ask risk owners at different levels to describe the organization's risk appetite and compare with the formal statement
Review recent risk acceptance decisions to verify they reference tolerance criteria
Check whether risk appetite has been recalibrated after significant incidents or business changes

GV.RM-03.title: Integration with Enterprise Risk Management
GV.RM-03.assessorFocus: Assess whether cybersecurity risks are aggregated with and managed alongside other enterprise risks, and whether cybersecurity risk managers participate in enterprise risk management processes.

evidenceItems.name: Enterprise Risk Register with Cybersecurity Risks
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that cybersecurity risks are included in the enterprise-wide risk register alongside other risk categories
whatGoodLooksLike: Cybersecurity risks appear in the enterprise risk register with consistent scoring methodology
Risk register enables comparison and prioritization across risk domains
Cybersecurity risks are escalated to enterprise level when they meet escalation criteria
commonGaps: Cybersecurity maintains a separate risk register that is not integrated with enterprise risk
Risk scoring methodologies differ between cybersecurity and enterprise risk management
Cybersecurity risks are underrepresented in enterprise risk discussions
suggestedSources: Enterprise risk management team
GRC platform or risk register system

evidenceItems.name: ERM Committee Participation Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that cybersecurity risk managers participate in enterprise risk management planning and governance
whatGoodLooksLike: CISO or cybersecurity risk representative is a standing member of the ERM committee
Meeting minutes show active cybersecurity risk discussion at the enterprise level
Cybersecurity risk input is solicited for enterprise-level risk assessments
commonGaps: Cybersecurity team is not represented in enterprise risk governance forums
Participation is ad hoc rather than formalized
Cybersecurity provides reports but does not actively participate in risk discussions
suggestedSources: ERM committee charter and meeting minutes
Organizational governance structure documentation

evidenceItems.name: Cybersecurity Risk Escalation Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Documented criteria and process for escalating cybersecurity risks to enterprise risk management
whatGoodLooksLike: Clear escalation criteria based on risk severity, business impact, or tolerance threshold breach
Defined escalation paths with timelines and responsible parties
Evidence of escalation procedures being exercised
commonGaps: No formal escalation criteria or process exists
Escalation relies on informal relationships rather than defined procedures
Escalation procedures exist but have never been tested or used
suggestedSources: Risk management procedures documentation
Incident and escalation records

assessmentTips: Review the enterprise risk register to confirm cybersecurity risks are present and scored consistently with other risk domains
Interview the ERM committee chair to understand the level of cybersecurity integration
Ask for examples of cybersecurity risks that were escalated to enterprise level and how they were handled

GV.RM-04.title: Risk Response Strategy and Direction
GV.RM-04.assessorFocus: Verify that the organization has established and communicated strategic direction for risk response options (accept, avoid, mitigate, transfer) with clear criteria for each.

evidenceItems.name: Risk Response Framework and Criteria
evidenceItems.description: Documented framework defining criteria for selecting risk response options across different scenarios
whatGoodLooksLike: Clear criteria for when to accept, avoid, mitigate, or transfer cybersecurity risks
Criteria are differentiated by data classification, asset criticality, or risk severity
Framework is approved by senior leadership and communicated to risk owners
commonGaps: Risk response decisions are made ad hoc without standardized criteria
Framework exists but is not applied consistently across the organization
No differentiation of response criteria based on asset or data sensitivity
suggestedSources: Cybersecurity risk management policy and procedures
Enterprise risk management framework

evidenceItems.name: Risk Transfer and Insurance Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Records of risk transfer decisions including cybersecurity insurance policies and shared responsibility agreements
whatGoodLooksLike: Active cybersecurity insurance policy with coverage aligned to identified risk exposure
Shared responsibility models are documented for cloud and third-party services
Risk transfer decisions are formally approved and documented
commonGaps: No cybersecurity insurance or coverage is inadequate relative to risk exposure
Shared responsibility models are not documented or understood
Risk transfer decisions are not formally tracked or reviewed
suggestedSources: Finance or insurance management team
Cloud and vendor management documentation

evidenceItems.name: Risk Acceptance Records
evidenceItems.description: Formal documentation of risks accepted by authorized personnel with supporting rationale
whatGoodLooksLike: Each risk acceptance is documented with rationale, approver, conditions, and expiration date
Risk acceptances are reviewed periodically to confirm conditions still hold
Acceptance authority is defined and limited to appropriate management levels
commonGaps: Risks are accepted informally without documentation
No expiration or review dates on risk acceptances
Acceptance authority is not defined, allowing inappropriate risk acceptance at lower levels
suggestedSources: Risk register or GRC platform
Risk acceptance approval records

assessmentTips: Review a sample of recent risk treatment decisions to verify they align with the documented response framework
Confirm that risk acceptance decisions were made at the appropriate authority level
Ask whether cybersecurity insurance coverage has been reviewed against current threat landscape and exposure

GV.RM-05.title: Cross-Organizational Risk Communication
GV.RM-05.assessorFocus: Assess whether formal communication lines are established across the organization for cybersecurity risks, including mechanisms for reporting to leadership and coordinating with all relevant departments.

evidenceItems.name: Cybersecurity Risk Communication Plan
evidenceItems.description: Documented plan defining communication channels, frequencies, audiences, and content for cybersecurity risk reporting
whatGoodLooksLike: Plan covers upward reporting to executives, lateral communication across departments, and downward to operational teams
Communication frequencies are defined (e.g., monthly dashboards to executives, quarterly board briefings)
Plan includes supplier and third-party risk communication channels
commonGaps: No formal communication plan exists; reporting is ad hoc
Communication is limited to IT and security teams without cross-departmental reach
Supplier and third-party risk communication is absent from the plan
suggestedSources: CISO office governance documentation
Corporate communications or governance team

evidenceItems.name: Executive and Board Cybersecurity Briefing Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of regular cybersecurity risk briefings to senior executives and the board
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular briefing cadence (at least quarterly) with documented agendas and minutes
Briefings include risk posture, key risk indicators, incidents, and strategic recommendations
Evidence of leadership questions, decisions, and action items resulting from briefings
commonGaps: Board briefings on cybersecurity are infrequent or superficial
Briefings focus on operational metrics without strategic risk context
No documentation of leadership decisions or actions taken based on briefings
suggestedSources: Board secretary or corporate governance office
CISO presentation archives

evidenceItems.name: Cross-Departmental Coordination Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of cybersecurity risk communication and coordination across organizational departments
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular cross-functional meetings include cybersecurity risk as a standing agenda item
Departments such as HR, legal, operations, and physical security actively participate
Communication records show two-way information flow between cybersecurity and other functions
commonGaps: Cybersecurity risk discussions are siloed within IT or security teams
Cross-departmental meetings exist but cybersecurity is not a regular topic
No mechanism for non-technical departments to report cybersecurity concerns
suggestedSources: Cross-functional committee meeting minutes
Internal collaboration platform records

assessmentTips: Interview heads of non-IT departments to assess their awareness of cybersecurity risk communication channels
Review the last four board briefings to evaluate depth and quality of cybersecurity risk reporting
Verify that risk communication includes third-party and supply chain risks

GV.RM-06.title: Standardized Risk Calculation and Categorization
GV.RM-06.assessorFocus: Verify that the organization uses a standardized, documented methodology for calculating, documenting, categorizing, and prioritizing cybersecurity risks, and that it is consistently applied.

evidenceItems.name: Risk Assessment Methodology Document
evidenceItems.description: Formal documentation of the organization's approach to calculating and scoring cybersecurity risks
whatGoodLooksLike: Methodology includes defined probability and impact scales with clear criteria for each level
Quantitative and/or qualitative approaches are specified with formulas or scoring rubrics
Methodology is consistently applied across the organization and reviewed periodically
commonGaps: No standardized methodology exists; risk scoring varies by team or assessor
Methodology exists but is not consistently applied across assessments
Probability and impact scales are undefined or subjective
suggestedSources: Risk management framework documentation
GRC platform configuration and methodology guides

evidenceItems.name: Risk Register with Standardized Entries
evidenceItems.description: Active risk register demonstrating consistent application of the standardized methodology
whatGoodLooksLike: All entries use the same scoring methodology with complete fields (description, likelihood, impact, rating, owner)
Risk categories are consistently applied from a predefined taxonomy
Register supports aggregation and comparison across organizational units
commonGaps: Risk register entries are inconsistent in format, scoring, or completeness
Multiple risk registers exist with incompatible formats
Risk categories are applied inconsistently or are undefined
suggestedSources: GRC platform or risk register system
Cybersecurity risk management team

evidenceItems.name: Risk Prioritization and Categorization Criteria
evidenceItems.description: Documented criteria for categorizing and prioritizing risks at appropriate enterprise levels
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk categories are defined in a taxonomy aligned with enterprise risk management
Prioritization criteria consider business impact, likelihood, and strategic relevance
Criteria support risk aggregation and reporting at different organizational levels
commonGaps: No formal risk taxonomy or categorization scheme exists
Prioritization is based solely on technical severity without business context
Criteria do not enable meaningful comparison across risk domains
suggestedSources: Risk management policy and procedures
Enterprise risk taxonomy documentation

assessmentTips: Compare risk entries from different assessments to verify consistent application of the methodology
Ask assessors to walk through how they score a risk to confirm understanding of the methodology
Verify that the risk register supports aggregation and enterprise-level reporting

GV.RM-07.title: Strategic Opportunities (Positive Risks)
GV.RM-07.assessorFocus: Assess whether the organization identifies and characterizes strategic opportunities (positive risks) in cybersecurity and includes them in risk management discussions alongside threats.

evidenceItems.name: Opportunity Identification and Analysis Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of strategic opportunities identified through cybersecurity risk management activities
whatGoodLooksLike: SWOT analyses or similar frameworks are used to identify cybersecurity-related opportunities
Positive risks are documented with potential benefits, likelihood, and required investment
Opportunities are categorized (e.g., competitive advantage, efficiency gain, trust building)
commonGaps: Risk management focuses exclusively on threats with no consideration of opportunities
No formal method for identifying or documenting positive risks
Cybersecurity is viewed solely as a cost center rather than a strategic enabler
suggestedSources: Strategic planning documentation
CISO or cybersecurity leadership reports

evidenceItems.name: Risk Discussion Records Including Positive Risks
evidenceItems.description: Meeting minutes or reports showing that positive risks are discussed alongside negative risks
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk committee agendas explicitly include opportunity assessment as a standing item
Positive risks are presented to leadership with the same rigor as threats
Decision records show opportunities being pursued based on risk-informed analysis
commonGaps: Risk committee discussions focus exclusively on threats and vulnerabilities
Opportunities are discussed informally but not captured in governance records
No examples of opportunities being pursued as a result of risk analysis
suggestedSources: Risk committee meeting minutes
Strategy review documentation

evidenceItems.name: Stretch Goals and Innovation Roadmap
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of cybersecurity stretch goals and innovation initiatives that leverage security as a differentiator
whatGoodLooksLike: Stretch goals are documented with timelines, resource requirements, and expected benefits
Innovation roadmap includes cybersecurity capabilities that enable business growth
Progress toward stretch goals is tracked and reported
commonGaps: No cybersecurity stretch goals or innovation objectives exist
Goals focus solely on compliance and threat reduction
No tracking or accountability for pursuing strategic opportunities
suggestedSources: CISO strategic plan or roadmap
Innovation or digital transformation program documentation

assessmentTips: Ask leadership to provide examples of cybersecurity capabilities that have created business value or competitive advantage
Review risk committee minutes for evidence that positive risks are regularly discussed
Determine whether the organization has pursued any cybersecurity-driven innovation in the past year

GV.RR-01.title: Leadership Accountability and Culture
GV.RR-01.assessorFocus: Verify that organizational leadership is responsible and accountable for cybersecurity risk, actively fosters a risk-aware and ethical culture, and directs the cybersecurity strategy.

evidenceItems.name: Leadership Cybersecurity Roles and Accountability Charter
evidenceItems.description: Formal documentation of leadership roles, responsibilities, and accountability for cybersecurity risk management
whatGoodLooksLike: Board and executive roles in cybersecurity governance are explicitly defined in a charter or policy
CISO reporting line provides direct access to senior leadership
Annual leadership review of cybersecurity strategy is mandated and documented
commonGaps: No formal assignment of cybersecurity accountability at the board or executive level
CISO reports deep within IT without direct leadership access
Leadership engagement is limited to incident response rather than proactive governance
suggestedSources: Board or governance committee charter
Organizational governance and reporting structure documentation

evidenceItems.name: Culture and Awareness Leadership Communications
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of leadership actively promoting a risk-aware and ethical cybersecurity culture
whatGoodLooksLike: Executive communications on cybersecurity expectations (e.g., all-hands messages, policy endorsements)
Leaders use real-world examples to reinforce positive or negative cybersecurity behaviors
Leadership participates visibly in security awareness activities
commonGaps: No evidence of leadership communicating cybersecurity expectations to the broader organization
Culture-building is delegated entirely to the security team without executive sponsorship
Communications are generic and infrequent
suggestedSources: Internal communications archive (emails, intranet posts)
Security awareness program documentation

evidenceItems.name: Authority and Coordination Review Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of periodic reviews ensuring adequate authority and coordination among cybersecurity risk management leaders
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual reviews assess whether cybersecurity leaders have sufficient authority and resources
Coordination gaps between security functions are identified and remediated
Review findings result in documented action items with follow-through
commonGaps: No periodic review of cybersecurity authority and coordination effectiveness
Authority gaps are known but not formally addressed
Reviews are conducted but findings are not acted upon
suggestedSources: Governance review reports
Internal audit findings related to cybersecurity governance

assessmentTips: Interview board members or executives to gauge their understanding of their cybersecurity accountability
Ask employees outside the security team about leadership tone-setting on cybersecurity
Review the CISO reporting structure and assess whether it enables effective escalation

GV.RR-02.title: Cybersecurity Roles and Responsibilities
GV.RR-02.assessorFocus: Confirm that cybersecurity risk management roles, responsibilities, and authorities are formally defined, communicated across the organization, understood by incumbents, and enforced through performance management.

evidenceItems.name: Cybersecurity RACI Matrix
evidenceItems.description: Responsibility assignment matrix defining who is Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, and Informed for cybersecurity activities
whatGoodLooksLike: RACI covers all key cybersecurity functions (risk management, incident response, compliance, architecture)
Matrix clearly distinguishes between operational, risk, and audit functions
Matrix is maintained as a living document and updated when organizational changes occur
commonGaps: No formal RACI or equivalent exists for cybersecurity functions
Roles overlap significantly without clear accountability delineation
Matrix is outdated and does not reflect current organizational structure
suggestedSources: CISO office governance documentation
HR or organizational design team

evidenceItems.name: Position Descriptions with Cybersecurity Responsibilities
evidenceItems.description: Job descriptions and role profiles that include cybersecurity responsibilities and performance requirements
whatGoodLooksLike: Cybersecurity responsibilities are explicitly stated in relevant position descriptions
Performance requirements include measurable cybersecurity objectives
Both dedicated security roles and roles with security-adjacent duties are covered
commonGaps: Position descriptions are generic and do not mention cybersecurity responsibilities
Cybersecurity duties are informal and not reflected in job documentation
Only dedicated security roles have cybersecurity in their descriptions; IT operations and business roles do not
suggestedSources: HR department position description repository
Cybersecurity team role documentation

evidenceItems.name: Cybersecurity Performance Assessment Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of performance measurement for personnel with cybersecurity responsibilities
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual performance reviews include evaluation of cybersecurity objectives
Performance metrics are specific and measurable for cybersecurity roles
Areas for improvement are identified with development plans
commonGaps: Cybersecurity performance is not evaluated in performance reviews
Performance goals exist but are not measured or tracked
No consequences or incentives tied to cybersecurity performance
suggestedSources: HR performance management system (anonymized/aggregated)
CISO team performance records

assessmentTips: Sample position descriptions across IT, security, and business units to verify cybersecurity responsibilities are included
Ask personnel in cybersecurity-adjacent roles to describe their security responsibilities and compare with documentation
Verify that the RACI matrix is actively used in decision-making, not just a shelf document

GV.RR-03.title: Adequate Resource Allocation
GV.RR-03.assessorFocus: Assess whether adequate resources (people, process, technology, and budget) are allocated to cybersecurity commensurate with the organization's risk strategy, responsibilities, and policies.

evidenceItems.name: Cybersecurity Budget and Resource Allocation Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of budget and resource allocation for cybersecurity risk management activities
whatGoodLooksLike: Dedicated cybersecurity budget is defined and aligned with risk strategy and identified needs
Budget allocation is traceable to risk priorities and strategic objectives
Resource allocation is reviewed and adjusted based on changing risk landscape
commonGaps: Cybersecurity budget is embedded within general IT budget without clear delineation
Resource allocation is not linked to risk assessment findings or strategy
Budget has remained flat despite increasing risk exposure or expanded responsibilities
suggestedSources: Finance or budget management team
CISO budget requests and approval records

evidenceItems.name: Staffing and Capability Assessment
evidenceItems.description: Assessment of cybersecurity staffing levels and skill capabilities against requirements
whatGoodLooksLike: Workforce planning analysis compares current staffing to required capabilities
Skill gaps are identified with corresponding hiring or training plans
Staffing levels are benchmarked against industry standards or peer organizations
commonGaps: No formal assessment of cybersecurity staffing adequacy exists
Known staffing gaps persist without remediation plans
Staffing decisions are reactive rather than based on strategic workforce planning
suggestedSources: CISO workforce planning documentation
HR talent management records

evidenceItems.name: Management Authority Review Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of periodic reviews confirming that cybersecurity personnel have necessary authority to fulfill their responsibilities
whatGoodLooksLike: Management reviews assess whether cybersecurity roles have sufficient decision-making authority
Authority gaps are identified and escalated for resolution
Reviews are conducted at least annually with documented outcomes
commonGaps: No formal review of whether cybersecurity staff have adequate authority
Authority limitations are known but not formally addressed
Reviews are conducted but do not result in meaningful changes
suggestedSources: Governance review or management review records
Internal audit reports on cybersecurity function effectiveness

assessmentTips: Compare cybersecurity budget trends against the growth in assets, threats, and regulatory requirements
Ask the CISO whether resource requests have been denied and how gaps are managed
Verify that resource allocation decisions are documented and linked to risk priorities

GV.RR-04.title: Cybersecurity in Human Resources Practices
GV.RR-04.assessorFocus: Verify that cybersecurity considerations are integrated into human resources lifecycle processes including screening, onboarding, role changes, training, and offboarding.

evidenceItems.name: HR-Security Integration Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedures for integrating cybersecurity into HR lifecycle processes
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedures cover screening, onboarding, role changes, and offboarding with cybersecurity checkpoints
Background check requirements are defined for sensitive roles with periodic re-screening
Automated workflows ensure timely access provisioning and deprovisioning
commonGaps: No formal integration between HR processes and cybersecurity requirements
Background checks are limited to initial hire without periodic re-screening
Offboarding process does not include timely access revocation or asset recovery
suggestedSources: HR department policies and procedures
Identity and access management team documentation

evidenceItems.name: Security Policy Acknowledgment Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that personnel acknowledge and agree to uphold cybersecurity policies
whatGoodLooksLike: All employees sign security policy acknowledgments at hire and annually thereafter
Acknowledgments are updated when policies change significantly
Non-compliance with acknowledgment requirements is tracked and escalated
commonGaps: No formal acknowledgment process exists for cybersecurity policies
Acknowledgments are collected at hire but not renewed annually
Contractors and temporary staff are not included in the acknowledgment process
suggestedSources: HR onboarding records
Learning management system or policy management platform

evidenceItems.name: Cybersecurity Training and Competency Records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that cybersecurity knowledge is developed and valued in hiring, training, and retention
whatGoodLooksLike: Role-based cybersecurity training is mandatory and completion is tracked
Cybersecurity competencies are included in hiring criteria for relevant roles
Training content is updated to reflect current threats and organizational policies
commonGaps: Training is generic and not tailored to role-specific cybersecurity responsibilities
Training completion rates are low with no enforcement mechanism
Cybersecurity knowledge is not a factor in hiring or promotion decisions
suggestedSources: Learning management system records
HR training and development documentation

assessmentTips: Review a sample of recent onboarding and offboarding cases to verify cybersecurity steps were executed
Check access revocation timeliness for recently departed employees
Verify that background check requirements scale with the sensitivity of the role

GV.PO-01.title: Cybersecurity Risk Management Policy
GV.PO-01.assessorFocus: Verify that a comprehensive cybersecurity risk management policy exists, is based on organizational context and strategy, has been approved by senior management, and is effectively communicated and enforced across the organization.

evidenceItems.name: Cybersecurity Risk Management Policy Document
evidenceItems.description: The master policy document establishing management intent, expectations, and direction for cybersecurity risk management
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy includes clear statements of management intent, scope, applicability, and enforcement
Policy is aligned with organizational context, risk strategy, and regulatory requirements
Policy is approved by senior management with documented approval date and signatures
commonGaps: Policy is outdated and does not reflect current organizational context or risk strategy
Policy lacks senior management approval or is approved only by IT leadership
Policy is overly technical and not understandable by non-technical stakeholders
suggestedSources: CISO office or cybersecurity governance team
Policy management system or document repository
evidenceItems.retentionPeriod: Current + all prior versions for 5 years

evidenceItems.name: Policy Communication and Dissemination Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the cybersecurity policy has been communicated to and acknowledged by all personnel
whatGoodLooksLike: Distribution records confirm policy was shared with all applicable personnel
Policy is accessible through the organization's intranet or policy portal
New hires receive and acknowledge the policy during onboarding
commonGaps: Policy exists but has not been formally communicated beyond the security team
No mechanism to confirm personnel have received and read the policy
Policy is not easily accessible to all employees
suggestedSources: Policy management platform or intranet
HR onboarding process documentation

evidenceItems.name: Policy Enforcement and Compliance Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the cybersecurity policy is enforced through monitoring, compliance checks, and corrective actions
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular compliance checks or audits verify adherence to policy requirements
Non-compliance incidents are documented with corrective actions
Enforcement mechanisms include escalation procedures and consequences for violations
commonGaps: Policy exists but there is no monitoring or enforcement mechanism
Non-compliance is identified but not formally addressed or tracked
Enforcement is inconsistent, with different standards for different personnel
suggestedSources: Internal audit or compliance team reports
Incident and corrective action records

assessmentTips: Verify the policy approval chain reaches senior management and not just IT leadership
Sample personnel acknowledgment records to confirm broad communication
Ask for examples of policy enforcement actions to confirm the policy is not just aspirational

GV.PO-02.title: Policy Review and Update Process
GV.PO-02.assessorFocus: Confirm that the cybersecurity risk management policy is subject to a defined review cycle and is updated to reflect changes in requirements, threats, technology, and organizational mission.

evidenceItems.name: Policy Review Schedule and Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of the policy review cycle, including scheduled reviews and evidence of execution
whatGoodLooksLike: Defined review cadence (at least annually) with documented review dates and outcomes
Review process involves relevant stakeholders including legal, compliance, and business leaders
Reviews are triggered both by schedule and by significant environmental changes
commonGaps: No defined review schedule exists; policy is updated only when problems arise
Reviews are scheduled but frequently deferred or skipped
Review process does not include input from non-technical stakeholders
suggestedSources: Policy management system or governance calendar
CISO office or governance team records

evidenceItems.name: Policy Change Log and Version History
evidenceItems.description: Record of all policy changes, the rationale for changes, and the triggers that prompted them
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive change log with dates, authors, change descriptions, and rationale
Changes are traceable to specific triggers (regulatory changes, incidents, technology adoption)
Version history is maintained with all prior versions accessible
commonGaps: No change log or version history is maintained
Changes are made without documenting the rationale or trigger
Only the current version is retained without access to prior versions
suggestedSources: Document management system or policy repository
Change management records

evidenceItems.name: Policy Update Communication Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that policy updates are communicated to all affected personnel in a timely manner
whatGoodLooksLike: Communication records show all affected personnel were notified of policy changes
Updated acknowledgments are collected following significant policy changes
Communication includes a summary of changes and their implications
commonGaps: Policy updates are published but not proactively communicated to personnel
No re-acknowledgment process exists for policy updates
Personnel are unaware of recent policy changes
suggestedSources: Internal communications archive
Policy management platform notification records

assessmentTips: Check the policy version history to confirm reviews occurred on schedule
Verify that recent regulatory changes or significant incidents triggered policy reviews
Ask personnel whether they are aware of the most recent policy changes and how they were notified

GV.OV-01.title: Strategy Outcome Review
GV.OV-01.assessorFocus: Verify that cybersecurity risk management strategy outcomes are periodically reviewed, and that findings are used to inform adjustments to strategy and organizational direction.

evidenceItems.name: Strategy Performance Review Reports
evidenceItems.description: Reports documenting periodic assessment of how well the cybersecurity risk management strategy achieves its objectives
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular reviews (at least annually) evaluate strategy effectiveness against defined objectives
Reviews measure how risk management outcomes supported leadership decision-making
Reports include recommendations for strategy adjustments
commonGaps: No formal strategy performance review process exists
Reviews focus on operational metrics without evaluating strategic effectiveness
Review findings are not translated into actionable strategy adjustments
suggestedSources: CISO office strategic review documentation
Governance committee review records

evidenceItems.name: Strategy Adjustment Decision Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that strategy reviews result in documented decisions to adjust or reaffirm the cybersecurity strategy
whatGoodLooksLike: Decision records show specific strategy adjustments made based on review findings
Adjustments are approved by appropriate leadership and communicated to stakeholders
Rationale for adjustments (or for maintaining current strategy) is documented
commonGaps: Reviews are conducted but do not result in documented decisions or changes
Strategy changes are made without formal review or approval process
No feedback loop exists between strategy outcomes and future planning
suggestedSources: Executive or governance committee meeting minutes
Strategy planning documentation

evidenceItems.name: Operational Impact Assessment of Strategy
evidenceItems.description: Assessment of whether cybersecurity risk strategies impede operations or innovation, with remediation where needed
whatGoodLooksLike: Feedback from operational teams is collected on the impact of security policies and controls
Overly restrictive controls that impede business objectives are identified and reviewed
Balance between security and operational efficiency is actively managed
commonGaps: No mechanism for operational teams to provide feedback on security strategy impacts
Security controls are implemented without considering operational impact
Known friction points between security and operations are not addressed
suggestedSources: Operations and business unit feedback records
Security exception or waiver request data

assessmentTips: Ask leadership what strategy adjustments were made in the past year based on performance reviews
Review exception and waiver requests as indicators of strategy-operations friction
Verify that strategy reviews incorporate feedback from across the organization, not just the security team

GV.OV-02.title: Strategy Coverage and Adjustment Review
GV.OV-02.assessorFocus: Assess whether the cybersecurity risk management strategy is periodically reviewed to ensure it adequately covers organizational requirements and emerging risks, with adjustments made as needed.

evidenceItems.name: Audit and Compliance Review Findings
evidenceItems.description: Audit findings that assess whether the cybersecurity strategy ensures compliance with internal and external requirements
whatGoodLooksLike: Internal and external audits assess strategy coverage against requirements
Audit findings are mapped to specific strategy gaps with remediation plans
Audit results inform strategic planning and resource allocation
commonGaps: Audits focus on control compliance without evaluating strategy adequacy
Audit findings are addressed tactically without informing strategic adjustments
No process to aggregate audit findings into strategic insights
suggestedSources: Internal audit department reports
External audit or assessment findings

evidenceItems.name: Post-Incident Strategy Review Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that cybersecurity incidents trigger review and potential adjustment of the risk management strategy
whatGoodLooksLike: Significant incidents include a lessons-learned phase that evaluates strategy adequacy
Strategy adjustments resulting from incident reviews are documented and implemented
Incident patterns are analyzed for systemic strategy weaknesses
commonGaps: Incident response focuses on tactical recovery without strategic review
Lessons learned are documented but do not feed into strategy adjustments
No process to evaluate whether incidents indicate strategy-level gaps
suggestedSources: Incident response post-mortem reports
Lessons learned documentation

evidenceItems.name: Personnel Performance and Role Oversight Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that performance of cybersecurity-related roles is reviewed to determine if policy changes are necessary
whatGoodLooksLike: Performance reviews of cybersecurity roles identify systemic issues requiring policy changes
Role effectiveness is assessed against strategy objectives
Identified gaps in role performance lead to documented policy or process improvements
commonGaps: Performance oversight of cybersecurity roles does not inform policy decisions
No mechanism to link individual or team performance gaps to strategy adjustments
Role performance is assessed only on operational metrics without strategic alignment
suggestedSources: HR performance management records (aggregated)
CISO management review documentation

assessmentTips: Trace a recent audit finding to determine whether it informed a strategy adjustment
Ask how the most recent significant cybersecurity incident influenced strategy review
Verify that strategy reviews consider emerging threats and changes in the organizational risk environment

GV.OV-03.title: Risk Management Performance Evaluation
GV.OV-03.assessorFocus: Verify that cybersecurity risk management performance is systematically evaluated using key performance indicators (KPIs) and key risk indicators (KRIs), and that results are communicated to leadership and used to drive adjustments.

evidenceItems.name: KPI and KRI Framework Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Formal documentation of key performance indicators and key risk indicators used to evaluate cybersecurity risk management
whatGoodLooksLike: KPIs measure policy and procedure effectiveness against defined objectives
KRIs identify emerging risks with defined thresholds for escalation
Indicators are reviewed and refined periodically to maintain relevance
commonGaps: No formal KPI or KRI framework exists for cybersecurity risk management
Indicators are purely operational (e.g., patch counts) without strategic risk context
KRIs lack defined thresholds or escalation triggers
suggestedSources: Cybersecurity metrics and measurement documentation
GRC platform or security dashboard configuration

evidenceItems.name: Cybersecurity Risk Management Performance Reports
evidenceItems.description: Periodic reports presenting KPI and KRI data to senior leadership with analysis and recommendations
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular reporting cadence (at least quarterly) with trend analysis
Reports include both current state and trajectory toward objectives
Leadership receives actionable recommendations based on performance data
commonGaps: Performance data is collected but not regularly reported to leadership
Reports present raw data without analysis, context, or recommendations
Reporting is inconsistent in format, frequency, or content
suggestedSources: CISO reporting archives
Executive briefing presentation materials

evidenceItems.name: Performance-Driven Adjustment Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that performance evaluation results drive specific adjustments to cybersecurity risk management
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented decisions showing performance data was used to justify resource, strategy, or control changes
Action items from performance reviews are tracked to completion
Continuous improvement cycle is evident with measurable outcomes
commonGaps: Performance evaluations are conducted but do not result in changes
No tracking mechanism for actions resulting from performance reviews
Improvement initiatives are initiated but not measured for effectiveness
suggestedSources: Governance committee action item trackers
Continuous improvement or management review records

assessmentTips: Request the last four quarterly performance reports and assess whether they show improvement trends
Verify that KRIs have defined thresholds and ask for examples of threshold-triggered escalations
Ask leadership how they use cybersecurity performance data in their decision-making

GV.SC-01.title: Supply Chain Risk Management Program
GV.SC-01.assessorFocus: Verify that a formal cybersecurity supply chain risk management (C-SCRM) program exists with a defined strategy, objectives, policies, processes, and stakeholder agreement.

evidenceItems.name: C-SCRM Program Charter and Strategy
evidenceItems.description: Formal documentation establishing the supply chain risk management program, its objectives, and strategic direction
whatGoodLooksLike: Program charter defines scope, objectives, governance structure, and stakeholder responsibilities
Strategy includes milestones, maturity targets, and alignment with enterprise risk management
Stakeholders from cybersecurity, IT, procurement, legal, and operations have agreed to the program
commonGaps: No formal C-SCRM program exists; supply chain risk is managed ad hoc
Program exists on paper but lacks stakeholder buy-in or resources for implementation
Strategy does not include measurable objectives or milestones
suggestedSources: CISO office or supply chain risk management team
Procurement and vendor management leadership

evidenceItems.name: C-SCRM Policies and Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Policies and procedures that guide the implementation of supply chain risk management activities
whatGoodLooksLike: Policies cover supplier assessment, contracting, monitoring, and offboarding
Procedures are practical and actionable with clear responsibilities
Policies are aligned with the overall cybersecurity and enterprise risk management frameworks
commonGaps: Policies are generic and do not address supply chain-specific cybersecurity risks
Procedures exist but are not followed consistently across the organization
No cross-functional alignment between cybersecurity, procurement, and legal policies
suggestedSources: Policy management system
Procurement policy documentation

evidenceItems.name: Cross-Functional Alignment and Coordination Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that supply chain risk management is coordinated across relevant organizational functions
whatGoodLooksLike: Cross-functional committee or working group addresses C-SCRM with regular meetings
Roles and responsibilities are clearly defined across cybersecurity, procurement, legal, and operations
Meeting records show active collaboration and decision-making
commonGaps: C-SCRM is siloed within procurement without cybersecurity involvement
No formal cross-functional coordination mechanism exists
Coordination is ad hoc and triggered only by incidents
suggestedSources: C-SCRM working group charter and meeting minutes
Organizational governance documentation

assessmentTips: Ask stakeholders from different functions to describe their role in the C-SCRM program
Verify the program has dedicated resources and is not just a policy without implementation
Check whether the program strategy has been reviewed and updated since its initial creation

GV.SC-02.title: Supply Chain Roles and Responsibilities
GV.SC-02.assessorFocus: Confirm that cybersecurity roles and responsibilities for suppliers, customers, and partners are formally defined, communicated both internally and externally, and coordinated across the organization.

evidenceItems.name: Supply Chain RACI Matrix
evidenceItems.description: Responsibility matrix defining cybersecurity roles for supply chain risk management across internal and external parties
whatGoodLooksLike: RACI covers internal teams (security, procurement, legal) and external parties (suppliers, partners)
Shared cybersecurity responsibilities are explicitly documented and agreed upon
Matrix is integrated into vendor management and procurement processes
commonGaps: No formal RACI exists for supply chain cybersecurity responsibilities
Internal roles are defined but supplier responsibilities are vague
Responsibility matrix is not communicated to suppliers or partners
suggestedSources: C-SCRM program documentation
Procurement or vendor management team

evidenceItems.name: Third-Party Cybersecurity Responsibility Agreements
evidenceItems.description: Documented agreements with suppliers and partners defining their cybersecurity responsibilities
whatGoodLooksLike: Supplier agreements include specific cybersecurity obligations and accountability provisions
Information sharing rules and reporting protocols are defined in agreements
Agreements are standardized across supplier relationships with criticality-based customization
commonGaps: Supplier contracts do not include cybersecurity responsibility provisions
Cybersecurity obligations are vague or unenforceable in agreements
No standardized approach to defining supplier cybersecurity responsibilities
suggestedSources: Legal department contract templates
Vendor management agreement repository

evidenceItems.name: Internal Communication of Third-Party Roles
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that supply chain cybersecurity roles and responsibilities are communicated internally to relevant teams
whatGoodLooksLike: Internal teams understand their responsibilities for managing supplier cybersecurity risks
Communication records show distribution of role definitions to procurement, IT, and business units
Performance goals for C-SCRM roles are documented and tracked
commonGaps: Supply chain roles are defined but not communicated to operational teams
Business units engage suppliers without awareness of cybersecurity responsibility requirements
No performance goals or accountability for C-SCRM responsibilities
suggestedSources: Internal communications and training records
C-SCRM program awareness materials

assessmentTips: Interview procurement staff to assess their understanding of cybersecurity responsibilities in supplier management
Review sample supplier agreements to verify cybersecurity obligations are present and enforceable
Ask how information sharing between the organization and suppliers is governed and monitored

GV.SC-03.title: Supply Chain Risk Integration
GV.SC-03.assessorFocus: Assess whether cybersecurity supply chain risk management is integrated into the broader cybersecurity and enterprise risk management programs, including risk assessment and continuous improvement processes.

evidenceItems.name: Integrated Risk Management Framework Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that supply chain cybersecurity risks are managed within the enterprise risk management framework
whatGoodLooksLike: C-SCRM risks are assessed using the same methodology and appear in the enterprise risk register
Integrated control sets address both general cybersecurity and supply chain risks
Risk assessment processes explicitly include supply chain scenarios
commonGaps: Supply chain risks are managed separately from enterprise risk management
Risk assessment methodology does not include supply chain risk scenarios
No integration between cybersecurity controls and supply chain-specific controls
suggestedSources: Enterprise risk management framework documentation
Risk assessment templates and completed assessments

evidenceItems.name: Supply Chain Risk Escalation Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that material supply chain cybersecurity risks are escalated to senior management and enterprise risk forums
whatGoodLooksLike: Escalation criteria for supply chain risks are defined and documented
Records show supply chain risks being escalated to enterprise risk management when material
Senior management reviews and acts on escalated supply chain risks
commonGaps: No escalation criteria exist for supply chain cybersecurity risks
Supply chain risks are managed at the operational level without senior management visibility
Escalation occurs only during incidents rather than proactively
suggestedSources: Risk escalation records and governance meeting minutes
Enterprise risk committee documentation

evidenceItems.name: C-SCRM Continuous Improvement Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that supply chain risk management processes are subject to continuous improvement
whatGoodLooksLike: Improvement initiatives are identified through risk assessments, audits, and incident reviews
Improvement actions are tracked with measurable outcomes
Lessons learned from supply chain incidents are incorporated into program improvements
commonGaps: No continuous improvement process exists for C-SCRM
Improvements are reactive and driven only by incidents
Improvement actions are identified but not tracked or measured
suggestedSources: C-SCRM program review documentation
Continuous improvement or corrective action trackers

assessmentTips: Review the enterprise risk register for the presence and scoring of supply chain cybersecurity risks
Ask for an example of a supply chain risk that was escalated to enterprise risk management
Verify that C-SCRM improvements are tracked as part of the broader cybersecurity improvement program

GV.SC-04.title: Supplier Criticality Assessment
GV.SC-04.assessorFocus: Verify that the organization maintains a comprehensive inventory of suppliers, has established criticality criteria, and prioritizes suppliers based on their assessed criticality.

evidenceItems.name: Supplier Inventory and Criticality Register
evidenceItems.description: Comprehensive register of all suppliers with criticality classifications based on defined criteria
whatGoodLooksLike: All suppliers are inventoried with basic information (services provided, data access, system connectivity)
Criticality ratings are assigned based on documented criteria
Register is maintained as a living document updated when supplier relationships change
commonGaps: Supplier inventory is incomplete, missing informal or shadow vendor relationships
Criticality ratings are not applied or are applied inconsistently
Register is static and not updated as supplier relationships evolve
suggestedSources: Vendor management system or procurement records
CISO or C-SCRM team documentation

evidenceItems.name: Supplier Criticality Assessment Criteria
evidenceItems.description: Documented criteria used to determine the criticality of each supplier relationship
whatGoodLooksLike: Criteria include data sensitivity, system access level, service importance to mission, and replaceability
Criteria are approved by appropriate stakeholders and consistently applied
Criteria are reviewed and updated periodically to reflect organizational changes
commonGaps: No formal criticality criteria exist; prioritization is based on contract value alone
Criteria do not consider cybersecurity-specific factors like data access or system connectivity
Criteria have not been updated to reflect changes in the organization's risk profile
suggestedSources: C-SCRM program documentation
Vendor risk assessment procedures

evidenceItems.name: Supplier Prioritization and Tiering Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that suppliers are prioritized into tiers with differentiated risk management activities per tier
whatGoodLooksLike: Suppliers are organized into tiers (e.g., critical, high, medium, low) with defined management expectations
Higher-tier suppliers receive more rigorous assessment, monitoring, and contractual requirements
Tiering decisions are documented with supporting rationale
commonGaps: All suppliers receive the same level of oversight regardless of criticality
Tiering exists but does not drive differentiated risk management activities
Prioritization is informal and not documented
suggestedSources: Vendor management program documentation
Procurement or C-SCRM team records

assessmentTips: Cross-reference the supplier inventory with accounts payable data to identify unlisted suppliers
Verify that criticality criteria include cybersecurity factors, not just financial or operational ones
Ask how a new supplier is assessed and classified to confirm the process is operational

GV.SC-05.title: Contractual Cybersecurity Requirements
GV.SC-05.assessorFocus: Confirm that cybersecurity risk requirements are established, prioritized, and systematically integrated into contracts and agreements with suppliers and third parties based on their criticality.

evidenceItems.name: Standard Contractual Cybersecurity Clauses
evidenceItems.description: Template contract language that includes cybersecurity requirements for suppliers and third parties
whatGoodLooksLike: Standard clauses address security controls, incident notification, audit rights, and data protection
Clauses are tiered based on supplier criticality and data sensitivity
Legal and cybersecurity teams jointly develop and maintain contract language
commonGaps: No standard cybersecurity clauses exist in supplier contracts
Clauses are generic and not tailored to the risk level of the supplier relationship
Cybersecurity requirements are added inconsistently depending on who manages the contract
suggestedSources: Legal department contract templates
Procurement standard terms and conditions

evidenceItems.name: Supplier Compliance Verification Requirements
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of how supplier compliance with contractual cybersecurity requirements is verified
whatGoodLooksLike: Contracts specify compliance verification methods (self-attestation, certifications, audits, inspections)
Verification frequency is proportionate to supplier criticality
SBOM or component inventory requirements are included for critical product suppliers
commonGaps: Contracts require cybersecurity compliance but specify no verification mechanism
Verification relies solely on self-attestation without independent validation
No requirements for software or hardware bill of materials from product suppliers
suggestedSources: Contract management system
Vendor risk assessment program documentation

evidenceItems.name: SLA Cybersecurity Performance Requirements
evidenceItems.description: Service level agreements that include cybersecurity performance metrics and monitoring requirements
whatGoodLooksLike: SLAs define measurable cybersecurity performance targets (e.g., incident response times, uptime)
Consequences for non-compliance with cybersecurity SLAs are specified
SLA performance is monitored throughout the supplier relationship lifecycle
commonGaps: SLAs focus on availability and performance without cybersecurity-specific metrics
No consequences are defined for cybersecurity SLA breaches
SLA monitoring does not include cybersecurity performance indicators
suggestedSources: Service level agreement documentation
Vendor performance management records

assessmentTips: Sample contracts across different supplier tiers to verify cybersecurity clauses are present and proportionate
Ask how compliance with contractual cybersecurity requirements is actually monitored and enforced
Verify that SBOM requirements are included for suppliers of critical software or hardware products

GV.SC-06.title: Supplier Due Diligence
GV.SC-06.assessorFocus: Assess whether the organization performs thorough cybersecurity due diligence on prospective suppliers before entering formal relationships, with rigor proportionate to the criticality and risk of the relationship.

evidenceItems.name: Supplier Due Diligence Procedures and Checklists
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedures for conducting cybersecurity due diligence on prospective suppliers
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedures define due diligence steps scaled to the risk level and criticality of the supplier relationship
Checklists cover cybersecurity capabilities, certifications, incident history, and risk management practices
Due diligence is a required gate before contract execution
commonGaps: No formal due diligence procedure exists for cybersecurity assessment of suppliers
Due diligence is limited to financial viability without cybersecurity evaluation
Due diligence is optional or can be bypassed for urgent procurements
suggestedSources: Procurement procedures documentation
C-SCRM program operational guides

evidenceItems.name: Completed Supplier Risk Assessments
evidenceItems.description: Completed cybersecurity risk assessments for prospective and current suppliers
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk assessments are completed before contract execution and cover cybersecurity-specific criteria
Assessment depth is proportionate to supplier criticality and risk exposure
Assessment findings include risk ratings and recommended mitigations or contract conditions
commonGaps: Risk assessments are conducted after contracts are signed, limiting remediation options
All suppliers receive the same assessment regardless of criticality
Assessment findings do not influence contract terms or decision to engage
suggestedSources: Vendor risk assessment repository
GRC platform supplier assessment records

evidenceItems.name: Product Authenticity and Integrity Verification Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the authenticity and security of critical products are assessed prior to acquisition and deployment
whatGoodLooksLike: Critical products are verified for authenticity through supply chain provenance checks
Security testing or evaluation is performed on critical products before deployment
Verification results are documented and inform procurement decisions
commonGaps: No product authenticity or integrity verification process exists
Verification is limited to commercial off-the-shelf products without assessing custom or integrated solutions
Products are deployed without security evaluation
suggestedSources: IT security or engineering team evaluation records
Procurement acceptance testing documentation

assessmentTips: Review the procurement workflow to confirm cybersecurity due diligence is a mandatory gate
Sample completed supplier assessments across criticality tiers to verify proportionate rigor
Ask what happens when a prospective supplier fails the cybersecurity due diligence assessment

GV.SC-07.title: Ongoing Supplier Risk Monitoring
GV.SC-07.assessorFocus: Verify that supplier risks are continuously understood, recorded, assessed, and monitored throughout the relationship lifecycle, with appropriate risk response actions taken.

evidenceItems.name: Supplier Ongoing Assessment and Monitoring Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of periodic cybersecurity assessments and continuous monitoring of suppliers during the relationship
whatGoodLooksLike: Assessment frequency is proportionate to supplier criticality (e.g., annual for critical, biennial for others)
Multiple monitoring methods are used (audits, self-attestations, certifications, continuous monitoring feeds)
Changes in supplier risk profile trigger reassessment
commonGaps: Assessments are performed only at onboarding with no ongoing monitoring
Monitoring relies solely on supplier self-attestation without independent verification
Changes in supplier risk profile go undetected until an incident occurs
suggestedSources: Vendor risk management system
Supplier audit and assessment reports

evidenceItems.name: Supplier Compliance Evidence Repository
evidenceItems.description: Repository of third-party evidence of compliance with contractual cybersecurity requirements
whatGoodLooksLike: Suppliers provide regular compliance evidence (SOC 2 reports, ISO certifications, penetration test results)
Evidence is reviewed and evaluated by qualified personnel
Non-compliance findings are tracked with required remediation timelines
commonGaps: Compliance evidence is collected but not reviewed or evaluated
Only a subset of suppliers provide compliance evidence
No tracking of non-compliance findings or remediation progress
suggestedSources: Vendor management compliance repository
GRC platform third-party risk module

evidenceItems.name: Supply Chain Business Continuity Plans
evidenceItems.description: Plans addressing unexpected supplier interruptions to ensure business continuity
