commonGaps: Penetration tests are limited to external network scans without internal or application-layer testing
Test scope excludes critical systems due to business concerns without risk-based justification
Findings are reported but not formally tracked to remediation with verified closure
suggestedSources: Penetration test and red team engagement reports
Remediation tracking records for pentest findings
Scope approval documentation and rules of engagement

evidenceItems.name: Tabletop Exercise and Simulation After-Action Reports
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization conducts tabletop exercises and simulations for incident response, business continuity, and disaster recovery, with lessons learned driving improvements.
whatGoodLooksLike: Exercises are conducted at least annually with participation from cross-functional stakeholders including senior executives
After-action reports document observed gaps, lessons learned, and specific improvement actions
Improvement actions from exercises are assigned owners, deadlines, and tracked to completion
commonGaps: Exercises are limited to the IT or security team without involvement of business leaders, legal, or HR
After-action reports identify issues but do not assign specific improvement actions or owners
Exercises use generic scenarios that do not reflect the organization's actual threat landscape
suggestedSources: Tabletop exercise scenarios, participant lists, and after-action reports
Business continuity and disaster recovery exercise reports
Improvement action items derived from exercises with tracking status

evidenceItems.name: Supplier and Third-Party Joint Exercise Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that security tests and exercises are conducted in coordination with critical suppliers and third parties to validate cross-organizational response capabilities.
whatGoodLooksLike: Joint exercises with critical suppliers are conducted at least annually covering supply chain disruption scenarios
Exercise findings identify improvements needed in cross-organizational communication and coordination
Supplier participation and improvement commitments are tracked and followed up
commonGaps: No joint exercises are conducted with suppliers; testing is purely internal
Supplier participation in exercises is requested but not required or tracked
Supply chain disruption scenarios are not included in the exercise program
suggestedSources: Joint exercise reports with supplier participants documented
Supply chain incident response exercise scenarios and after-action reviews
Contractual requirements for supplier participation in exercises

assessmentTips: Review after-action reports from recent exercises to verify that identified improvements were assigned, tracked, and implemented.
Ask how exercise scenarios are developed and whether they incorporate current threat intelligence and real-world incident trends.
Verify that penetration test scope covers the organization's most critical systems and is not limited by convenience.
Check whether the organization conducts exercises with critical suppliers and how supplier-side improvements are tracked.

ID.IM-03.title: Improvement from Operational Execution
ID.IM-03.assessorFocus: Verify the organization identifies cybersecurity improvements from the execution of day-to-day operational processes, procedures, and activities, including lessons learned from incidents, operational metrics, and supplier collaboration.

evidenceItems.name: Lessons Learned and Post-Incident Review Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization conducts structured lessons-learned reviews after incidents and significant operational events, with findings driving process improvements.
whatGoodLooksLike: Post-incident reviews are conducted for all significant incidents with documented root cause analysis and improvement actions
Lessons learned sessions include cross-functional participants and, where relevant, supplier representatives
Improvement actions are tracked to implementation with verified effectiveness
commonGaps: Post-incident reviews are conducted only for major incidents, missing improvement opportunities from smaller events
Lessons learned are documented but not translated into specific process or control changes
Suppliers are not included in lessons-learned sessions even when they contributed to the incident
suggestedSources: Post-incident review reports with improvement action items
Lessons learned meeting minutes and participant records
Improvement implementation tracking for incident-driven changes

evidenceItems.name: Operational Cybersecurity Metrics and Performance Reports
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization collects and analyzes operational cybersecurity metrics to identify trends, inefficiencies, and improvement opportunities.
whatGoodLooksLike: Defined set of key performance indicators (KPIs) and key risk indicators (KRIs) are collected and reported regularly
Metrics cover operational areas such as MTTR, patch compliance, alert volume, false positive rates, and access review completion
Metrics are analyzed for trends and used to justify process improvements and resource requests
commonGaps: Metrics are collected but not analyzed for trends or used to drive improvement decisions
Only compliance-oriented metrics are tracked while operational effectiveness metrics are absent
No defined thresholds or targets for metrics that would trigger improvement actions
suggestedSources: Security operations dashboard and periodic metric reports
KPI/KRI definition document with targets and thresholds
Management presentations using metrics to justify improvements

evidenceItems.name: Annual Policy and Procedure Review Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that cybersecurity policies, processes, and procedures are reviewed at least annually to incorporate lessons learned from operational execution.
whatGoodLooksLike: All cybersecurity policies and procedures have a defined review cycle with documented review and approval dates
Review process incorporates input from operational teams, incident findings, and metric trends
Changes made during reviews are tracked with rationale linked to lessons learned or operational findings
commonGaps: Policies are reviewed on paper but no substantive changes are made to reflect operational lessons
Review cycle is not consistently followed, with some policies years overdue for review
Operational staff who execute the procedures are not consulted during the review process
suggestedSources: Policy review logs showing review dates, reviewers, and changes made
Policy version history with change annotations
Procedure update records linked to operational improvement findings

assessmentTips: Ask for specific examples of operational improvements that were implemented as a result of lessons learned from incidents or operational metrics.
Review the last two annual policy review cycles to verify that reviews resulted in meaningful updates, not just date changes.
Check whether the organization conducts collaborative lessons-learned sessions with critical suppliers on a regular basis.
Verify that operational metrics have defined thresholds that trigger investigation and improvement action when breached.

ID.IM-04.title: Cybersecurity Plan Establishment and Maintenance
ID.IM-04.assessorFocus: Confirm that incident response plans, business continuity plans, disaster recovery plans, vulnerability management plans, and other cybersecurity plans are formally established, communicated to responsible parties, maintained current, and improved based on lessons learned.

evidenceItems.name: Cybersecurity Contingency Plans
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates formally established and approved cybersecurity plans including incident response, business continuity, disaster recovery, and vulnerability management plans.
whatGoodLooksLike: Plans are formally documented, approved by senior leadership, and cover incident response, BC/DR, and vulnerability management
Plans include contact information, communication protocols, escalation criteria, and scenario-specific procedures
Plans address both common and sector-specific scenarios relevant to the organization's threat profile
commonGaps: Plans exist for incident response but business continuity and disaster recovery plans are missing or outdated
Plans are generic templates that have not been tailored to the organization's specific environment and risks
Contact information and escalation procedures are outdated or incomplete
suggestedSources: Incident response plan, business continuity plan, disaster recovery plan documents
Vulnerability management plan
Plan approval records and distribution lists

evidenceItems.name: Plan Communication and Training Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that cybersecurity plans are communicated to all responsible parties and that personnel are trained on their roles and responsibilities within the plans.
whatGoodLooksLike: Plans are distributed to all personnel with defined roles and receipt acknowledgment is recorded
Training on plan execution is conducted at least annually for personnel with incident response or BC/DR responsibilities
Plan updates are communicated promptly to affected parties when changes occur
commonGaps: Plans are stored in a repository but not actively distributed or communicated to responsible parties
Training on plan execution is limited to the security team and does not include business stakeholders or executives
Plan updates are published without notification to personnel whose roles are affected
suggestedSources: Plan distribution and acknowledgment records
Training completion records for incident response and BC/DR exercises
Communication records for plan updates and changes

evidenceItems.name: Plan Review and Improvement Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that cybersecurity plans are reviewed and updated at least annually or when significant changes or improvement needs are identified, incorporating lessons learned from exercises and incidents.
whatGoodLooksLike: Plans are reviewed at least annually with documented review dates, reviewers, and changes made
Plan updates incorporate lessons learned from incidents, exercises, and operational experience
Significant organizational changes (mergers, new systems, regulatory changes) trigger out-of-cycle plan reviews
commonGaps: Plans have not been reviewed or updated in over 12 months
Reviews are cosmetic (date changes only) without substantive incorporation of lessons learned
No trigger mechanism exists for out-of-cycle reviews when significant changes occur
suggestedSources: Plan version history showing review dates and change summaries
After-action reports with plan improvement recommendations cross-referenced to plan updates
Change log documenting specific modifications and rationale

assessmentTips: Verify that all required cybersecurity plans exist, are current, and are approved by appropriate leadership -- not just the incident response plan.
Check whether plan contact information and escalation procedures are current by spot-checking a sample of entries.
Ask how lessons learned from incidents and exercises are formally incorporated into plan updates, looking for specific examples.
Verify that the vulnerability management plan is a standalone document or clearly defined section, not just ad hoc patching procedures.

PR.AA-01.title: Identity and Credential Management
PR.AA-01.assessorFocus: Verify the organization has a comprehensive identity lifecycle process covering provisioning, modification, and deprovisioning of credentials for users, services, and hardware assets.

evidenceItems.name: Identity Lifecycle Management Policy and Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates the organization has formal policies governing credential issuance, management, and revocation for all entity types
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy covers users, service accounts, and hardware identities with defined ownership
Procedures include timely deprovisioning triggered by HR separation events within 24 hours
Cryptographic key and certificate management lifecycle is documented with rotation schedules
commonGaps: Service accounts and non-human identities excluded from lifecycle management
No integration between HR termination process and access revocation workflow
Cryptographic key rotation not enforced or tracked
suggestedSources: Identity Governance and Administration (IGA) platform exports
HR-to-IT provisioning workflow documentation and audit logs
Certificate and key management system inventory reports

evidenceItems.name: Access Request and Approval Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows that access provisioning follows a formal request, review, and approval workflow with data/system owner authorization
whatGoodLooksLike: All access requests are tracked in a ticketing system with approver sign-off from resource owners
Requests include business justification and defined access scope
Automated workflows enforce approval chains before provisioning
commonGaps: Access granted informally via email or chat without formal approval records
No system or data owner involvement in approval decisions
Bulk provisioning bypasses individual request workflows
suggestedSources: ServiceNow or ITSM access request ticket exports
IGA platform approval workflow logs
Active Directory or IdP provisioning audit trails

evidenceItems.name: Hardware Identity Inventory and Labeling Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that authorized hardware assets are uniquely identified, labeled, and tracked using immutable characteristics or securely provisioned identifiers
whatGoodLooksLike: Hardware asset inventory includes unique identifiers tied to immutable hardware characteristics such as MAC address or TPM
Physical labels match inventory records and are verified during periodic audits
Device certificates or machine identities are provisioned and managed centrally
commonGaps: No correlation between physical labels and logical identity records
BYOD and IoT devices excluded from hardware identity management
Device certificates issued manually without centralized tracking
suggestedSources: CMDB or asset management platform exports
Endpoint management (Intune, JAMF) device inventory reports
PKI or device certificate management system logs

assessmentTips: Request a sample of recent joiner, mover, and leaver events and trace them through the full identity lifecycle
Verify that service accounts and machine identities are included in governance processes, not just human users
Check for orphaned accounts by comparing IdP user lists against active HR employee rosters
Ask how cryptographic credentials such as API keys, certificates, and tokens are inventoried and rotated

PR.AA-02.title: Identity Proofing and Credential Binding
PR.AA-02.assessorFocus: Confirm that the organization verifies claimed identities before binding them to credentials, using context-appropriate proofing methods and ensuring unique credential assignment.

evidenceItems.name: Identity Proofing Policy and Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates the organization has defined identity proofing requirements proportional to the risk of the access being granted
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy specifies proofing levels (e.g., NIST SP 800-63A IAL1/2/3) matched to access risk tiers
Government-issued ID verification is required for enrollment of privileged and high-risk users
Remote identity proofing uses trusted third-party verification services
commonGaps: Same proofing level applied regardless of access risk or data sensitivity
No documented proofing standard for contractors or third-party users
Self-service enrollment lacks identity verification beyond email confirmation
suggestedSources: Identity proofing policy and enrollment procedures documentation
Third-party identity verification service contracts and SLAs
IdP enrollment configuration and workflow documentation

evidenceItems.name: Enrollment and Credential Issuance Audit Logs
evidenceItems.description: Shows that identity proofing was performed and credentials were uniquely issued to verified individuals
whatGoodLooksLike: Audit logs capture proofing method used, verifier identity, and timestamp for each enrollment
Each individual receives unique credentials with no shared accounts
Credential issuance records are linked to verified identity proofing events
commonGaps: Enrollment logs do not record which proofing method was used
Shared or generic accounts exist without individual attribution
No audit trail linking a credential back to the proofing event
suggestedSources: IdP enrollment and credential issuance logs
HR onboarding records with ID verification checklists
Identity verification platform transaction logs

evidenceItems.name: Unique Credential Assignment Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that every user has a unique credential and shared credential usage is prohibited or tightly controlled
whatGoodLooksLike: Directory or IdP report shows one-to-one mapping between individuals and accounts
Policy explicitly prohibits credential sharing with enforcement controls
Exceptions for shared accounts are documented with compensating controls and individual accountability
commonGaps: Shared service or admin accounts used without individual attribution
No monitoring or detection of credential sharing behavior
Contractor accounts reused across different individuals over time
suggestedSources: Active Directory or IdP user account inventory reports
PAM platform shared account checkout and session logs
Acceptable use policy sections on credential sharing

assessmentTips: Request documentation of the proofing process for different user populations such as employees, contractors, and partners
Verify that remote proofing methods use trusted verification services rather than self-attestation alone
Sample recent enrollments and confirm proofing evidence was collected before credential issuance
Check whether shared or generic accounts exist and whether they have compensating controls for individual accountability

PR.AA-03.title: Authentication of Users, Services, and Hardware
PR.AA-03.assessorFocus: Validate that the organization enforces strong authentication for all entity types, including multifactor authentication, password policies, and periodic reauthentication aligned with risk.

evidenceItems.name: Authentication Policy and MFA Configuration
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization has defined and enforced authentication requirements including MFA and password strength
whatGoodLooksLike: MFA is required for all remote access, privileged access, and access to sensitive systems
Password policies enforce minimum complexity, length, and rotation aligned with current NIST guidance
Phishing-resistant MFA methods (FIDO2, hardware tokens) are used for high-risk access
commonGaps: MFA not enforced for service accounts, VPN, or legacy applications
Password policies rely on frequent rotation instead of length and complexity
SMS-based MFA used as the sole second factor for privileged access
suggestedSources: IdP conditional access and MFA policy configuration exports
Password policy settings from Active Directory or IdP
Authentication policy documentation

evidenceItems.name: Authentication Audit Logs and Metrics
evidenceItems.description: Shows authentication events including successful logins, failed attempts, lockouts, and reauthentication enforcement
whatGoodLooksLike: Logs capture authentication method, source IP, device posture, and success/failure status
Failed authentication attempts trigger lockout after a defined threshold
Periodic reauthentication is enforced for long-running sessions based on risk context
commonGaps: Authentication logs lack detail on method used or device context
No account lockout policy or lockout thresholds set too high
Session timeouts not enforced allowing indefinite authenticated sessions
suggestedSources: SIEM authentication event reports
IdP sign-in logs and conditional access audit reports
VPN and remote access gateway authentication logs

evidenceItems.name: Emergency Access and Break-Glass Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that authorized personnel can access critical accounts under emergency conditions through controlled break-glass procedures
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented break-glass procedure with step-by-step instructions and authorized personnel list
Break-glass accounts are monitored and generate alerts on use
Post-incident review is required after every break-glass event including credential rotation
commonGaps: No formal break-glass procedure documented or tested
Break-glass credentials stored insecurely or shared without monitoring
No post-use review or credential rotation after break-glass access
suggestedSources: Break-glass procedure documentation and authorized personnel register
PAM platform emergency access logs
Incident response records involving break-glass account usage

assessmentTips: Verify MFA enforcement by reviewing conditional access policy configurations, not just stated policy
Test whether legacy protocols that bypass MFA such as IMAP, SMTP, or LDAP simple bind are disabled
Ask about reauthentication triggers and validate session timeout configurations
Request evidence of break-glass procedure testing and post-use review records

PR.AA-04.title: Identity Assertion Protection and Verification
PR.AA-04.assessorFocus: Assess whether identity assertions used in SSO, federation, and cross-domain authentication are protected through signing, encryption, and proper verification practices.

evidenceItems.name: Federation and SSO Architecture Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization uses standards-based identity federation with proper assertion protection
whatGoodLooksLike: Architecture diagrams show SAML, OIDC, or OAuth 2.0 flows with assertion signing and encryption
Identity provider and relying party trust relationships are documented and reviewed
Token lifetimes are configured to minimize exposure and require refresh
commonGaps: Assertions transmitted without digital signatures or encryption
Federation trust relationships established without periodic review or revalidation
Token lifetimes set excessively long without refresh or revocation mechanisms
suggestedSources: SSO and federation architecture diagrams and design documents
IdP configuration exports showing signing and encryption settings
OAuth/OIDC/SAML relying party trust configuration documentation

evidenceItems.name: Assertion Signing and Encryption Configuration
evidenceItems.description: Shows that identity assertions are digitally signed and encrypted using current cryptographic standards
whatGoodLooksLike: SAML assertions are signed with SHA-256 or stronger and encrypted at the assertion level
OIDC tokens are signed with RS256 or ES256 and validated by relying parties
Certificate rotation for signing keys is scheduled and tracked
commonGaps: Assertions signed with deprecated algorithms such as SHA-1
Relying parties configured to accept unsigned assertions
Signing certificate rotation not planned or executed
suggestedSources: IdP SAML metadata showing signing certificate and algorithm configuration
OIDC well-known configuration endpoint exports
Certificate management records for assertion signing keys

evidenceItems.name: Assertion Verification and Validation Logs
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that relying parties validate assertion signatures, check expiration, and reject invalid tokens
whatGoodLooksLike: Application logs show signature verification performed on every received assertion
Invalid or expired assertions are rejected and logged as security events
Audience restriction and issuer validation are enforced at each relying party
commonGaps: Relying parties do not validate assertion signatures or audience restrictions
No logging of assertion validation failures
Replay attacks possible due to missing nonce or timestamp validation
suggestedSources: Application-level authentication logs showing assertion validation
Web application firewall or API gateway token validation logs
Security testing reports covering assertion handling

assessmentTips: Review actual IdP and relying party configurations rather than relying solely on architecture documents
Verify that assertion signing uses current cryptographic algorithms and that SHA-1 is not in use
Check that token lifetimes and refresh policies are appropriate for the risk level of the applications
Ask whether penetration testing has included assertion manipulation, replay, and injection attacks

PR.AA-05.title: Access Permissions and Least Privilege Enforcement
PR.AA-05.assessorFocus: Evaluate whether access permissions are defined in policy, enforce least privilege and separation of duties, and are reviewed periodically with prompt revocation of unnecessary privileges.

evidenceItems.name: Access Control Policy and Role Definitions
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that access permissions are defined in policy with role-based or attribute-based access models enforcing least privilege
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented RBAC or ABAC model with role definitions mapped to job functions
Least privilege and separation of duties principles explicitly stated and enforced in policy
Access control policies address physical and logical access across all platforms including cloud
commonGaps: Access roles are overly broad or not aligned with actual job responsibilities
Separation of duties not enforced for critical business functions such as financial approvals
Cloud resource access permissions not governed by the same policies as on-premises
suggestedSources: Access control policy and role definition documentation
RBAC or ABAC model design documents with role-to-permission mappings
Cloud IAM policy exports from AWS, Azure, or GCP

evidenceItems.name: Access Review and Recertification Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows that access privileges are periodically reviewed by resource owners with documented decisions to retain or revoke
whatGoodLooksLike: Quarterly or semi-annual access reviews completed by resource owners with documented accept/revoke decisions
Revocations executed within defined SLA after review decisions
Privileged access reviews conducted at higher frequency than standard user reviews
commonGaps: Access reviews are performed as a rubber-stamp exercise without meaningful owner evaluation
Revocation decisions identified during review are not acted upon timely
Privileged and service accounts excluded from periodic review campaigns
suggestedSources: IGA platform access certification campaign reports
Access review decision records with reviewer signatures
Remediation tracking records for revoked access

evidenceItems.name: Separation of Duties Matrix and Enforcement Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that incompatible duties are identified and technical or procedural controls prevent their combination
whatGoodLooksLike: SoD matrix defines incompatible role combinations for critical business processes
Technical controls in ERP or IAM systems prevent assignment of conflicting roles
SoD violations are detected, reported, and resolved through exception management
commonGaps: No formal SoD matrix exists or it has not been updated for current roles
SoD enforcement relies on manual processes without automated detection
Known SoD violations accepted without compensating controls or time-bound remediation
suggestedSources: SoD conflict matrix documentation
ERP or IAM system SoD violation detection reports
Exception management records for approved SoD conflicts

assessmentTips: Compare actual entitlements of a sample of users against their role definitions to verify least privilege
Request evidence of access revocation for recently departed or role-changed personnel
Evaluate whether access reviews are substantive by checking revocation rates and reviewer time-on-task
Verify that SoD controls extend to cloud environments and not just on-premises ERP systems

PR.AA-06.title: Physical Access Management and Monitoring
PR.AA-06.assessorFocus: Assess whether physical access to facilities and assets is managed, monitored, and enforced commensurate with the risk of the assets housed within, including visitor management and high-security zones.

evidenceItems.name: Physical Access Control Policy and Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization has defined physical access requirements proportional to asset risk for all facility types
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy defines physical access zones with escalating controls for higher-risk areas such as data centers and server rooms
Visitor management procedures require escort, sign-in, and badge issuance
Physical access provisioning and revocation follows a formal workflow tied to employment status
commonGaps: Physical access policy does not differentiate between general office areas and high-security zones
Visitor escort requirements not consistently enforced
Physical access badges not revoked promptly upon termination
suggestedSources: Physical security policy and facility access procedures
Visitor management policy and sign-in log templates
Physical access provisioning workflow documentation

evidenceItems.name: Physical Access Control System Logs and Reports
evidenceItems.description: Shows that physical access events are logged, monitored, and periodically reviewed for anomalies
whatGoodLooksLike: Badge reader logs capture all entry and exit events with timestamps and individual identification
After-hours and unauthorized access attempts generate real-time alerts
Physical access logs are retained and reviewed as part of security monitoring
commonGaps: Physical access logs not integrated with security monitoring or SIEM
Tailgating and piggybacking not detected or monitored
No periodic review of physical access logs for anomalous patterns
suggestedSources: Physical access control system (PACS) event logs
Security camera and alarm system monitoring reports
SIEM physical access event correlation reports

evidenceItems.name: Physical Access Review and Recertification Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that physical access privileges are periodically reviewed and aligned with current personnel status and need
whatGoodLooksLike: Periodic reviews of physical access badges verify all holders are current authorized personnel
Access to high-security zones is reviewed at higher frequency with facility manager approval
Lost or stolen badges are reported and deactivated immediately with replacement tracking
commonGaps: Physical access reviews not conducted or conducted only for logical access
Former employees or contractors retain active physical access badges
No process for tracking and deactivating lost or stolen badges
suggestedSources: Physical access review records with reviewer attestations
Badge inventory reconciliation reports
Lost or stolen badge incident reports

assessmentTips: Walk the facility to verify that physical controls match documented policy including locked doors, cameras, and badge readers
Cross-reference physical access badge holders against current HR roster to identify orphaned badges
Test visitor management by observing sign-in process and escort compliance
Verify that high-security areas such as data centers and network closets have enhanced controls beyond standard office access

PR.AT-01.title: General Cybersecurity Awareness and Training
PR.AT-01.assessorFocus: Evaluate whether all personnel including employees, contractors, and third-party users receive general cybersecurity awareness training covering common threats, policies, and cyber hygiene, with periodic assessment of knowledge retention.

evidenceItems.name: Security Awareness Training Program Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates the organization has a structured awareness training program covering all user populations
whatGoodLooksLike: Training program covers social engineering, phishing, password hygiene, acceptable use, and incident reporting
Annual refresher training is mandated with new content reflecting current threat landscape
Training is required for all employees, contractors, and third-party users before system access is granted
commonGaps: Contractors and third-party users excluded from training requirements
Training content is static and not updated to reflect evolving threats
No consequences defined for non-completion of mandatory training
suggestedSources: Security awareness training program charter and curriculum
Training platform configuration and content catalog
Policy mandating training completion with enforcement mechanisms

evidenceItems.name: Training Completion Records and Compliance Reports
evidenceItems.description: Shows that training was completed by all required personnel with tracking of compliance rates
whatGoodLooksLike: Completion rates at or above 95% for all user populations with escalation for non-compliance
Individual completion records with dates, module names, and assessment scores
Management dashboards track completion rates by department and user type
commonGaps: Low completion rates without management escalation or enforcement
Completion records do not distinguish between initial and refresher training
No tracking mechanism for contractor or third-party training completion
suggestedSources: LMS training completion reports by department and user type
Non-compliance escalation records
Quarterly training compliance dashboards

evidenceItems.name: Phishing Simulation and Knowledge Assessment Results
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization periodically tests user awareness through simulations and assessments
whatGoodLooksLike: Phishing simulations conducted at least quarterly with trending metrics on click rates
Users who fail simulations receive targeted remedial training
Knowledge assessment pass rates demonstrate comprehension of key security concepts
commonGaps: Phishing simulations not conducted or conducted infrequently
No remedial training for users who repeatedly fail simulations
Assessment results not used to improve training content or frequency
suggestedSources: Phishing simulation platform campaign reports and trend data
Post-training knowledge assessment score reports
Remedial training assignment and completion records

assessmentTips: Verify training content is current by reviewing the last update date and checking for coverage of recent threats
Review phishing simulation trend data to assess whether awareness is improving over time
Confirm that training is completed before system access is provisioned for new users
Check whether training addresses organization-specific policies such as data classification and acceptable use

PR.AT-02.title: Specialized Role-Based Cybersecurity Training
PR.AT-02.assessorFocus: Confirm that individuals in specialized roles such as IT administrators, security personnel, developers, finance staff, and senior leadership receive targeted training beyond general awareness that addresses the cybersecurity risks specific to their responsibilities.

evidenceItems.name: Specialized Role Training Matrix and Curriculum
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization has identified roles requiring specialized training and mapped appropriate curricula to each role
whatGoodLooksLike: Training matrix maps specialized roles to required cybersecurity courses and certifications
Role-based training covers topics specific to each role such as secure coding for developers and incident response for SOC analysts
Annual refreshers and updated content are required to address evolving role-specific threats
commonGaps: Specialized roles not formally identified or limited to IT security staff only
Senior leadership and finance staff not included in role-based training requirements
Training content is generic and not tailored to the specific risks of each role
suggestedSources: Role-based training matrix with role-to-course mappings
Specialized training curriculum and course descriptions
HR or learning team role classification documents

evidenceItems.name: Specialized Training Completion and Certification Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows that individuals in specialized roles completed required training and hold relevant certifications
whatGoodLooksLike: Completion records demonstrate role-specific training was delivered and assessed
Relevant professional certifications are tracked and maintained for security and IT staff
Third-party contractors in specialized roles meet the same training requirements
commonGaps: No differentiation between general awareness completion and specialized role training
Certifications not tracked or allowed to expire without renewal
Third-party contractors exempt from specialized training requirements
suggestedSources: LMS role-based training completion reports
Professional certification tracking register
Vendor or contractor training compliance records

evidenceItems.name: Role-Based Training Effectiveness Assessment
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization evaluates whether specialized training translates into improved security practices for each role
whatGoodLooksLike: Post-training assessments or practical exercises validate skill acquisition for specialized roles
Tabletop exercises and red team scenarios test trained competencies in realistic conditions
Training effectiveness metrics inform curriculum updates and investment decisions
commonGaps: No post-training assessment beyond simple completion tracking
Tabletop exercises and hands-on drills not conducted for specialized roles
Training program not updated based on assessment results or lessons learned
suggestedSources: Post-training assessment and practical exercise results
Tabletop exercise after-action reports
Annual training program effectiveness review reports

assessmentTips: Verify that specialized roles are broadly defined beyond just IT security to include finance, HR, executives, and developers
Request evidence of hands-on or scenario-based training rather than only slide-deck awareness modules
Check whether training requirements for specialized roles are enforced as prerequisites for role assignment
Review whether training content reflects current threats and techniques relevant to each specialized role

PR.DS-01.title: Data-at-Rest Protection
PR.DS-01.assessorFocus: Verify that the organization protects the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of stored data through encryption, integrity validation, and controls on removable media across all storage environments.

evidenceItems.name: Data-at-Rest Encryption Policy and Configuration
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization has defined encryption requirements for stored data and implemented them across all relevant platforms
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy mandates encryption for sensitive data at rest using current cryptographic standards such as AES-256
Full disk encryption is enabled on all endpoints with centralized key management
Database and cloud storage encryption is configured and verified across all environments
commonGaps: Encryption requirements not defined by data classification level
Legacy systems or databases store sensitive data without encryption
Encryption key management is decentralized or keys are stored alongside encrypted data
suggestedSources: Data encryption policy and standards documentation
Endpoint management platform encryption compliance reports
Cloud storage and database encryption configuration exports

evidenceItems.name: Data Integrity Validation and Software Verification Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows that the organization validates integrity of stored data and software through cryptographic hashes and digital signatures
whatGoodLooksLike: Software installations are verified against publisher-provided digital signatures before deployment
File integrity monitoring detects unauthorized changes to critical system and data files
Container images and VM disk images are signed and verified before deployment
commonGaps: No file integrity monitoring for critical data files and configuration files
Software signature verification not enforced during deployment
Container images deployed from untrusted or unverified registries
suggestedSources: File integrity monitoring (FIM) tool configuration and alert reports
Software deployment pipeline signature verification logs
Container registry image signing and scanning reports

evidenceItems.name: Removable Media Controls and Physical Security
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that removable media usage is controlled and physical security measures protect unencrypted sensitive data
whatGoodLooksLike: Removable media usage is restricted via endpoint DLP policies to prevent data exfiltration
Approved removable media devices are encrypted and registered in the asset inventory
Physical storage areas for sensitive media are secured with locked cabinets and access logs
commonGaps: No technical controls blocking unauthorized USB or removable device usage
Removable media with sensitive data not encrypted or inventoried
Physical security for media storage not enforced or audited
suggestedSources: Endpoint DLP policy configuration for removable media controls
Approved removable media device inventory
Physical media storage access logs and audit records

assessmentTips: Verify encryption at rest by examining actual platform configurations, not just policy statements
Check that key management practices include key rotation, separation from encrypted data, and escrow for recovery
Review file integrity monitoring coverage to ensure critical system files and sensitive data stores are included
Test removable media controls by requesting evidence of DLP enforcement and any exception records

PR.DS-02.title: Data-in-Transit Protection
PR.DS-02.assessorFocus: Assess whether the organization protects data in transit through encryption, prevents unauthorized data exfiltration, and controls the use of sensitive data across production and non-production environments.

evidenceItems.name: Data-in-Transit Encryption Standards and Configuration
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that all network communications carrying sensitive data use current encryption protocols
whatGoodLooksLike: TLS 1.2 or higher enforced for all external and internal communications with deprecated protocols disabled
VPN tunnels use strong encryption for remote access and site-to-site connections
Certificate management ensures valid, non-expired certificates on all services
commonGaps: Legacy protocols such as TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1, or SSLv3 still enabled on some services
Internal east-west traffic not encrypted between application tiers
Self-signed or expired certificates in use on internal services
suggestedSources: TLS configuration scan results from tools such as SSL Labs or internal scanners
Network security architecture showing encryption boundaries
Certificate inventory and validity reports

evidenceItems.name: Data Loss Prevention Controls for Communications
evidenceItems.description: Shows that outbound communications are monitored and controlled to prevent unauthorized disclosure of sensitive data
whatGoodLooksLike: DLP policies detect and block sensitive data in outbound email, web uploads, and cloud sharing
Automated encryption or blocking applied based on data classification labels
DLP incidents are logged, investigated, and tracked to resolution
commonGaps: DLP policies cover email only and do not extend to web, cloud, or endpoint channels
No data classification labels to trigger DLP enforcement
DLP alerts generate excessive false positives leading to alert fatigue and ignored incidents
suggestedSources: DLP platform policy configuration and channel coverage documentation
DLP incident reports with investigation and resolution details
Email gateway encryption and blocking rule configurations

evidenceItems.name: Production Data Segregation Controls
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that sensitive production data is prevented from being reused in development, testing, or other non-production environments
whatGoodLooksLike: Data masking or anonymization applied before production data is used in non-production environments
Policy prohibits direct copy of production data to dev/test without approval and sanitization
Automated pipelines enforce data sanitization as part of environment refresh processes
commonGaps: Production databases cloned directly to development environments without masking
No policy or technical control governing production data use in non-production
Data sanitization is manual and inconsistently applied
suggestedSources: Data masking and anonymization tool configuration and run reports
Data handling policy sections on non-production data usage
Environment refresh process documentation with sanitization steps

assessmentTips: Run or request recent TLS scan results against externally facing and critical internal services
Verify DLP coverage extends beyond email to include web, cloud storage, and endpoint channels
Ask for evidence of how production data is handled when refreshing dev/test environments
Check whether personal communication services such as personal email and consumer cloud storage are blocked on corporate networks

PR.DS-10.title: Data-in-Use Protection
PR.DS-10.assessorFocus: Verify that the organization protects data while being actively processed, including clearing sensitive data from memory when no longer needed and isolating data across processes and users on shared platforms.

evidenceItems.name: Data-in-Use Protection Architecture and Controls
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization has implemented technical controls to protect data during active processing
whatGoodLooksLike: Memory protection mechanisms prevent unauthorized access to data in use by other processes or users
Sensitive data is cleared from memory, caches, and temporary storage as soon as processing completes
Confidential computing or secure enclave technologies are used for high-sensitivity workloads
commonGaps: No documented approach to protecting data in use beyond standard OS protections
Sensitive data persists in memory, swap files, or temp directories after processing
Multi-tenant environments lack process-level isolation for sensitive data
suggestedSources: Security architecture documents covering data-in-use protections
Platform hardening guides addressing memory and process isolation
Secure coding standards covering sensitive data handling in memory

evidenceItems.name: Input Validation and Runtime Integrity Controls
evidenceItems.description: Shows that applications enforce input validation and runtime integrity protections to prevent data corruption and injection during processing
whatGoodLooksLike: Applications enforce input validation and output encoding to prevent injection attacks
Runtime application self-protection or integrity monitoring detects processing anomalies
Secure coding practices verified through code review and application security testing
commonGaps: Input validation implemented inconsistently across applications
No runtime monitoring for application-level data integrity violations
Secure coding practices not enforced or verified through testing
suggestedSources: Application security testing (SAST/DAST) reports covering input validation
Secure coding standards and code review checklists
RASP or application monitoring tool configuration and alert reports

evidenceItems.name: Data Access Monitoring During Processing
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that access to data during active processing is monitored and controlled
whatGoodLooksLike: Database activity monitoring captures queries and data access during processing
Application-level audit logs record data access operations with user context
Anomalous data access patterns during processing trigger alerts
commonGaps: No database activity monitoring or application-level data access logging
Monitoring covers only stored data access and not in-memory processing
Alerts for anomalous data access patterns not configured or not actioned
suggestedSources: Database activity monitoring (DAM) reports and configurations
Application audit log samples showing data access during processing
SIEM use cases and alerts for anomalous data access patterns

assessmentTips: Ask developers how sensitive data is handled in application memory and when it is cleared
Review secure coding standards for guidance on data-in-use protections such as zeroing buffers and avoiding logging sensitive data
Verify that multi-tenant or shared infrastructure implements process and memory isolation
Check whether database activity monitoring is in place for systems processing high-sensitivity data

PR.DS-11.title: Backup Management and Testing
PR.DS-11.assessorFocus: Evaluate whether backups are created for all critical data, protected from unauthorized access and ransomware, stored with geographic separation, and regularly tested for successful restoration.

evidenceItems.name: Backup Policy and Schedule Configuration
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization has defined backup requirements aligned with data criticality and recovery objectives
whatGoodLooksLike: Backup policy defines RPO and RTO targets for each data classification tier
Critical data backed up continuously or near-real-time with other data on defined schedules
Backup schedules are automated and monitored for completion with failure alerting
commonGaps: Backup schedules not aligned with defined RPO and RTO requirements
SaaS and cloud-native data not included in backup scope
Backup job failures not monitored or addressed in a timely manner
suggestedSources: Backup policy with RPO/RTO definitions per data tier
Backup platform schedule configuration and job reports
Backup monitoring dashboard showing success/failure rates

evidenceItems.name: Backup Protection and Storage Controls
evidenceItems.description: Shows that backups are protected from unauthorized modification, deletion, and ransomware through offline/offsite storage and immutability controls
whatGoodLooksLike: Immutable backup copies maintained with write-once-read-many or air-gapped storage
Backup storage is geographically separated from primary data with enforced geolocation restrictions
Backup encryption applied in transit and at rest with keys managed separately from primary systems
commonGaps: All backups stored on the same network or system as production data
No immutability or air-gapped copies to protect against ransomware
Backup encryption keys stored alongside backup data
suggestedSources: Backup storage architecture showing geographic separation and offline copies
Immutable storage configuration exports
Backup encryption configuration and key management documentation

evidenceItems.name: Backup Restoration Test Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that backups are regularly tested for successful restoration across all data source types
whatGoodLooksLike: Restoration tests conducted at least annually for all critical data sources with documented results
Test results confirm data integrity and restoration completes within defined RTO targets
Lessons learned from failed restoration tests drive improvements to backup processes
commonGaps: Restoration testing not conducted or limited to only a few data sources
Test results not documented or not verified for data integrity post-restoration
Restoration tests never measure time to restore against RTO targets
suggestedSources: Backup restoration test plans and execution reports
Restoration test results with data integrity verification evidence
Remediation records from failed restoration tests

assessmentTips: Verify that backup scope includes cloud services, SaaS platforms, and databases, not just on-premises file systems
Ask for evidence of immutable or air-gapped backup copies and test whether they can survive a simulated ransomware event
Review restoration test records and verify they cover all critical data types, not just a single test server
Confirm that backup monitoring alerts are configured and that recent failures were investigated and resolved

PR.PS-01.title: Configuration Management Practices
PR.PS-01.assessorFocus: Verify that the organization establishes, deploys, and monitors hardened configuration baselines for all platform types, with change control processes that prevent unauthorized deviations.

evidenceItems.name: Configuration Baseline Standards and Hardening Guides
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization has defined and documented secure configuration baselines for all platform types
whatGoodLooksLike: Hardened baselines defined for each platform type referencing CIS Benchmarks, DISA STIGs, or equivalent
Baselines enforce least functionality by disabling unnecessary services, ports, and protocols
Baselines are version-controlled and updated when new platform versions are deployed
commonGaps: No formal baselines defined or baselines based on vendor defaults without hardening
Baselines exist for servers but not for network devices, cloud services, or containers
Baselines not updated to reflect current platform versions or emerging threats
suggestedSources: Configuration baseline documents or CIS Benchmark implementation guides
Group Policy Objects, Ansible playbooks, or infrastructure-as-code templates
Configuration management database showing baseline versions and applicability

evidenceItems.name: Configuration Compliance Scanning Reports
evidenceItems.description: Shows that deployed systems are continuously monitored for deviations from approved configuration baselines
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated configuration compliance scans run at least weekly against approved baselines
Compliance reports show current posture with remediation tracking for non-compliant items
Coverage includes all platform types: servers, endpoints, network devices, cloud resources, and containers
commonGaps: Compliance scanning limited to a subset of systems or platform types
Scan results not tracked through remediation to closure
Scanning cadence is insufficient with monthly or quarterly scans only
suggestedSources: Configuration compliance scanning tool reports (e.g., Qualys, Nessus, AWS Config)
Non-compliance remediation tracking records
Compliance trend dashboards showing posture over time

evidenceItems.name: Change Management Records for Configuration Changes
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that configuration changes follow a formal change control process with testing, approval, and rollback planning
whatGoodLooksLike: All configuration changes require a change request with risk assessment and approval
Changes are tested in non-production before deployment with rollback plans documented
Emergency changes follow an expedited process with post-implementation review
commonGaps: Configuration changes made directly in production without change requests
No pre-deployment testing or rollback planning for configuration changes
Emergency change procedures not defined or emergency changes not reviewed after implementation
suggestedSources: Change management system records for configuration changes
Change Advisory Board meeting minutes and approval records
Post-implementation review reports for significant configuration changes

assessmentTips: Compare deployed configurations against baselines by requesting recent compliance scan results for a sample of systems
Verify that cloud resource configurations are included in baseline management and not just traditional servers
Review default configuration settings on recently deployed systems to confirm hardening was applied
Check that infrastructure-as-code templates enforce baselines and drift is detected automatically

PR.PS-02.title: Software Maintenance and Patch Management
PR.PS-02.assessorFocus: Evaluate whether the organization maintains software through timely patching, replaces end-of-life software, and removes unauthorized or unnecessary software to reduce attack surface.

evidenceItems.name: Vulnerability and Patch Management Policy and SLAs
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization has defined patching timelines based on vulnerability severity and asset criticality
whatGoodLooksLike: Patching SLAs defined by severity level: critical within 72 hours, high within 14 days, medium within 30 days
Emergency patching process enables rapid deployment for actively exploited vulnerabilities
End-of-life software inventory maintained with replacement or migration plans
commonGaps: Patching SLAs not defined or not differentiated by severity and asset criticality
No expedited process for zero-day or actively exploited vulnerabilities
End-of-life software running in production without documented risk acceptance or migration plan
suggestedSources: Vulnerability and patch management policy with SLA definitions
Software lifecycle and end-of-life tracking inventory
Emergency patching procedure documentation

evidenceItems.name: Patch Compliance and Deployment Reports
evidenceItems.description: Shows that patches are deployed within defined SLAs with tracking of compliance rates across the environment
whatGoodLooksLike: Patch compliance rates above 95% for critical and high severity patches within SLA timelines
Deployment reports show patching status across all OS, application, and firmware categories
Exceptions and deferrals documented with compensating controls and remediation timelines
commonGaps: Patching compliance measured only for OS patches and not application or firmware patches
Low patch compliance rates without management escalation
Systems excluded from patching due to operational concerns without formal risk acceptance
suggestedSources: Patch management platform compliance dashboards and reports
WSUS, SCCM, Intune, or equivalent patch deployment reports
Exception and deferral tracking records with risk acceptance

evidenceItems.name: Unauthorized and End-of-Life Software Removal Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that unauthorized or unnecessary software is identified and removed, and end-of-life software is replaced
whatGoodLooksLike: Software inventory scans identify unauthorized or unapproved software for removal
End-of-life software tracked with defined migration timelines and interim risk mitigation
Container images rebuilt with updated base images rather than patched in place
commonGaps: No automated discovery of unauthorized software installations
End-of-life software continues in production without replacement plans
Container images not rebuilt and continue running with outdated dependencies
suggestedSources: Software inventory audit reports identifying unauthorized software
End-of-life software remediation plans and status reports
Container image lifecycle management reports

assessmentTips: Request patch compliance data segmented by severity, asset type, and time-to-patch to assess SLA adherence
Check for end-of-life operating systems and applications in the software inventory and review replacement plans
Verify that third-party and open-source library patching is included, not just OS and first-party application patches
Ask how container images and serverless functions are maintained and updated

PR.PS-03.title: Hardware Maintenance and Lifecycle Management
PR.PS-03.assessorFocus: Confirm that hardware assets are maintained, replaced when they can no longer support required security capabilities, and disposed of securely with auditable records.

evidenceItems.name: Hardware Lifecycle Management Policy and Inventory
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization tracks hardware lifecycle status and has defined policies for maintenance, replacement, and disposal
whatGoodLooksLike: Hardware asset inventory includes acquisition date, warranty status, end-of-life date, and security capability assessment
Lifecycle policy defines replacement criteria based on security capability requirements
End-of-support hardware tracked with defined replacement timelines
commonGaps: Hardware inventory does not track lifecycle status or security capability limitations
No defined criteria for when hardware must be replaced due to security limitations
End-of-support hardware continues in production without replacement planning
suggestedSources: CMDB or asset management platform hardware lifecycle reports
Hardware lifecycle management policy documentation
End-of-life hardware replacement plans and status tracking

evidenceItems.name: Hardware Maintenance and Warranty Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows that hardware receives timely maintenance including firmware updates and that warranty and support coverage is maintained
whatGoodLooksLike: Firmware updates applied to hardware devices on a defined schedule
Maintenance contracts and warranties actively managed and renewed before expiration
Hardware maintenance performed by authorized personnel with audit trails
commonGaps: Firmware updates for network devices, IoT, and OT equipment not tracked or applied
Hardware operating past warranty without maintenance contracts
No audit trail for hardware maintenance activities
suggestedSources: Hardware firmware update compliance reports
Maintenance contract and warranty status register
Hardware maintenance activity logs

evidenceItems.name: Secure Hardware Disposal and Sanitization Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that hardware is disposed of securely with certified data sanitization and auditable chain-of-custody records
whatGoodLooksLike: Data sanitization performed using NIST SP 800-88 approved methods before disposal or reuse
Certificates of destruction provided by certified e-waste vendors for all disposed hardware
Chain-of-custody records maintained from decommissioning through final disposal
commonGaps: No formal data sanitization procedure or reliance on simple formatting instead of cryptographic erasure
Disposal vendor certificates not obtained or retained
Hardware disposed without chain-of-custody tracking allowing unaccounted assets
suggestedSources: Data sanitization and destruction certificates
Certified e-waste vendor contracts and audit reports
Hardware disposal tracking logs with chain-of-custody records

assessmentTips: Check the hardware inventory for devices past end-of-support and verify replacement plans exist
Request firmware update compliance data for network devices, which are frequently overlooked
Review disposal certificates and verify they reference specific serial numbers matching decommissioned assets
Ask whether hard drives from leased or returned hardware are sanitized before return to the vendor

PR.PS-04.title: Log Generation and Continuous Monitoring
PR.PS-04.assessorFocus: Verify that all operating systems, applications, and services generate log records that are securely transmitted to centralized logging infrastructure and made available for continuous security monitoring.

evidenceItems.name: Logging Policy and Standards
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization has defined what must be logged, log content requirements, and retention periods for all system types
whatGoodLooksLike: Logging policy specifies required log sources, event types, and minimum content fields per platform type
Retention periods defined based on regulatory requirements and security monitoring needs
Logging requirements address cloud services, containers, and SaaS platforms in addition to traditional infrastructure
commonGaps: Logging requirements not defined for cloud-native services, SaaS, or containerized workloads
Minimum log content fields not specified leading to inconsistent log quality
Retention periods not defined or not aligned with regulatory and business requirements
suggestedSources: Logging and monitoring policy and standards documentation
Log source inventory with required event types per source
Log retention schedule aligned with regulatory requirements

evidenceItems.name: Log Source Coverage and Collection Configuration
evidenceItems.description: Shows that log sources are configured to generate and securely transmit logs to centralized infrastructure with comprehensive coverage
whatGoodLooksLike: SIEM or log management platform shows ingestion from all critical log sources
Log collection uses encrypted transport and mutual authentication between sources and collectors
Log source coverage is tracked with gaps identified and remediated
commonGaps: Critical log sources such as DNS, DHCP, or cloud control plane not forwarded to SIEM
Log transport uses unencrypted syslog without integrity verification
No tracking of expected versus actual log sources to detect collection gaps
suggestedSources: SIEM log source inventory and ingestion status reports
Log collector configuration showing transport security settings
Log source coverage gap analysis reports

evidenceItems.name: Log Integrity and Availability Controls
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that logs are protected from tampering, deletion, and unauthorized access, and are available for analysis
whatGoodLooksLike: Logs are stored in append-only or immutable storage preventing tampering or deletion
Access to log management systems is restricted to authorized security personnel
Log availability is monitored with alerts for collection failures or gaps
commonGaps: Administrators of source systems can modify or delete their own audit logs
No immutability controls on log storage allowing post-facto tampering
Log collection failures not detected or alerted upon
suggestedSources: SIEM or log storage immutability and access control configuration
Log collection health monitoring dashboards and alerting configuration
Log access audit trails showing who accessed log data

assessmentTips: Compare the log source inventory against the asset inventory to identify systems not forwarding logs
Verify that cloud service logging such as AWS CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log, or GCP Audit Logs is enabled and forwarded
Check whether log retention meets both regulatory requirements and security investigation needs
Test log integrity by asking whether source system administrators can delete their own audit trails

PR.PS-05.title: Prevention of Unauthorized Software Execution
PR.PS-05.assessorFocus: Assess whether the organization prevents installation and execution of unauthorized software through application whitelisting, software source verification, and controlled DNS configurations.

evidenceItems.name: Application Control and Whitelisting Policy and Configuration
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization controls which software can be installed and executed on managed systems
whatGoodLooksLike: Application whitelisting enforced on critical systems with only approved executables permitted to run
Software installation restricted to approved sources such as enterprise app stores or managed deployment tools
Policy defines escalation path for requesting new software approval
commonGaps: Application control limited to blacklisting known malware rather than whitelisting approved software
Standard users retain local administrator privileges allowing unrestricted software installation
Application control not deployed on servers or only enforced on endpoints
suggestedSources: Application whitelisting tool configuration and policy exports (e.g., AppLocker, Carbon Black)
Software approval process documentation and approved software list
Endpoint management platform software restriction policy configuration

evidenceItems.name: Software Integrity Verification Controls
evidenceItems.description: Shows that the organization verifies the source and integrity of software before installation
whatGoodLooksLike: Digital signature verification required before software installation with unsigned code blocked
Software supply chain integrity verified through checksums and publisher certificates
Package managers configured to use only trusted repositories with signature verification enabled
commonGaps: Software installed from unverified internet sources without signature validation
Package manager repositories not restricted to trusted sources
No process for verifying integrity of open-source dependencies
suggestedSources: Code signing and signature verification policy and configuration
Package manager repository configuration showing trusted sources
Software bill of materials (SBOM) and dependency verification reports

evidenceItems.name: DNS Security and Malicious Domain Blocking Configuration
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that DNS resolution is controlled to block access to known malicious domains
whatGoodLooksLike: Managed DNS services configured to block resolution of known malicious and phishing domains
DNS-over-HTTPS and DNS-over-TLS used to prevent DNS hijacking and eavesdropping
All endpoints and servers configured to use only organization-approved DNS resolvers
commonGaps: No DNS-level blocking of malicious domains deployed
Endpoints can use arbitrary external DNS resolvers bypassing organizational controls
DNS security limited to on-premises and not extended to remote or cloud workloads
suggestedSources: Protective DNS service configuration and blocking reports
Network firewall rules restricting DNS traffic to approved resolvers
DNS query logs showing blocked malicious domain resolution attempts

assessmentTips: Test application whitelisting by asking whether an unapproved executable would be blocked on a sample system
Verify that local administrator privileges are removed from standard users to prevent unauthorized installations
Check whether DNS security controls extend to remote workers and cloud-hosted workloads
Review the approved software list process and verify it includes security review before approval

PR.PS-06.title: Secure Software Development Lifecycle
PR.PS-06.assessorFocus: Evaluate whether the organization integrates security practices throughout the software development lifecycle including secure coding, security testing, supply chain integrity, and secure disposal of retired software.

evidenceItems.name: Secure SDLC Policy and Framework Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization has adopted a secure SDLC framework with security gates integrated throughout the development process
whatGoodLooksLike: Secure SDLC framework adopted with security activities defined for each phase from design through deployment
Threat modeling performed during design phase for new applications and significant changes
Security requirements derived from risk assessments and integrated into user stories or specifications
commonGaps: Security activities bolt-on at the end of development rather than integrated throughout
No threat modeling performed during design phase
Security requirements not formally defined or tracked alongside functional requirements
suggestedSources: Secure SDLC policy and framework documentation
Threat modeling records for recent development projects
Security requirements documentation integrated into project backlogs

evidenceItems.name: Application Security Testing Results
evidenceItems.description: Shows that the organization conducts security testing throughout the development pipeline including SAST, DAST, SCA, and penetration testing
whatGoodLooksLike: SAST integrated into CI/CD pipeline with builds blocked on critical and high severity findings
DAST and penetration testing performed before production releases and on a recurring schedule
Software composition analysis identifies and tracks vulnerable open-source dependencies
commonGaps: Security testing performed only before major releases and not integrated into CI/CD pipeline
SAST findings not remediated before production deployment
Open-source dependency vulnerabilities not tracked or remediated
suggestedSources: SAST and DAST tool scan reports from recent releases
CI/CD pipeline configuration showing security gate enforcement
Software composition analysis reports and remediation tracking

evidenceItems.name: Source Code Protection and Supply Chain Integrity Controls
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that source code is protected from tampering and the software supply chain maintains integrity from development through deployment
whatGoodLooksLike: Source code repositories enforce branch protection, code review approval, and signed commits
Build pipelines use hardened runners with reproducible builds and artifact signing
Third-party dependencies pinned to specific versions with integrity verification
commonGaps: Source code repositories lack branch protection or allow direct pushes to main branches
Build pipeline integrity not assured with no artifact signing or reproducible builds
Third-party dependencies not pinned or verified allowing supply chain injection
suggestedSources: Source code repository access controls and branch protection configuration
CI/CD pipeline configuration showing build integrity controls
Dependency management and SBOM documentation

assessmentTips: Request SAST and DAST results from a recent release and verify that critical findings were resolved before deployment
Review CI/CD pipeline configuration to confirm security gates actually block deployments on policy violations
Ask about the process for managing and updating open-source dependencies and how vulnerabilities are tracked
Verify that source code repositories enforce code review and access controls appropriate for the sensitivity of the software

PR.IR-01.title: Network Protection from Unauthorized Access
PR.IR-01.assessorFocus: Verify that networks and environments are segmented according to trust boundaries, external communications are restricted to necessary traffic, and zero trust principles are applied to limit lateral movement.

evidenceItems.name: Network Segmentation Architecture and Design
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the network is segmented according to trust boundaries with enforcement of communication restrictions between segments
whatGoodLooksLike: Network architecture diagrams show segmentation by trust level (e.g., IT, OT, IoT, DMZ, guest) with defined allowed traffic flows
Micro-segmentation implemented for critical assets and sensitive data zones
External network boundaries enforce default-deny with only explicitly permitted traffic allowed inbound
commonGaps: Flat network architecture without meaningful segmentation between different trust levels
Segmentation exists at the perimeter but not between internal zones allowing unrestricted lateral movement
OT and IoT networks not segmented from IT networks
suggestedSources: Network architecture and segmentation design documents with traffic flow diagrams
Firewall and security group rule sets showing inter-segment access controls
Zero trust architecture implementation documentation

evidenceItems.name: Firewall and Access Control Rule Configuration
evidenceItems.description: Shows that network access controls enforce segmentation with explicit allow rules and default-deny posture
whatGoodLooksLike: Firewall rule bases follow default-deny with explicit permit rules documented with business justification
Rule sets are reviewed periodically with unused or overly permissive rules identified and remediated
Cloud security groups and network ACLs enforce segmentation in cloud environments
commonGaps: Firewall rules include broad any-to-any permits or excessively permissive rules
Firewall rule reviews not conducted or not resulting in rule cleanup
Cloud network security groups use default allow or overly permissive configurations
suggestedSources: Firewall rule set exports with justification documentation
Firewall rule review and cleanup records
Cloud security group and network ACL configuration exports

evidenceItems.name: Endpoint Health and Network Access Control
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that endpoint security posture is evaluated before granting network access to production resources
whatGoodLooksLike: Network access control (NAC) validates endpoint compliance with security policy before granting access
Non-compliant endpoints quarantined or given limited network access until remediated
Endpoint health checks include patch status, antimalware presence, and configuration compliance
commonGaps: No network access control or endpoint health validation before network access
NAC deployed only on wired networks and not wireless or VPN connections
Non-compliant endpoints allowed full network access without remediation enforcement
suggestedSources: NAC platform configuration and compliance check results
VPN gateway posture assessment configuration
Endpoint compliance reports correlated with network access decisions

assessmentTips: Request firewall rule sets and look for overly permissive rules such as any-any permits or wide IP ranges
Verify segmentation by tracing a hypothetical attacker's path from an endpoint to critical assets and identifying controls at each boundary
Check whether cloud environments have equivalent segmentation controls to on-premises networks
Ask whether endpoint health is validated for remote workers connecting via VPN or zero trust network access

PR.IR-02.title: Environmental Threat Protection
PR.IR-02.assessorFocus: Assess whether technology assets are protected from environmental threats including fire, flooding, power loss, and temperature extremes, with equivalent requirements for third-party hosted environments.

evidenceItems.name: Environmental Protection Design and Controls Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that facilities housing technology assets have appropriate environmental protections based on risk
whatGoodLooksLike: Data centers and server rooms equipped with fire suppression, flood detection, and climate control systems
Environmental monitoring systems provide real-time alerts for temperature, humidity, and water detection
Uninterruptible power supplies and backup generators ensure continued operation during power events
commonGaps: Network closets and secondary equipment rooms lack environmental protections
Environmental monitoring not configured to alert appropriate personnel
Backup power tested infrequently or capacity insufficient for full operational needs
suggestedSources: Facility environmental protection design documents and specifications
Environmental monitoring system configuration and alert routing
UPS and generator capacity planning and test records

evidenceItems.name: Environmental Systems Testing and Maintenance Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows that environmental protection systems are regularly tested and maintained to ensure operational readiness
whatGoodLooksLike: Fire suppression systems tested and inspected per code requirements with maintenance records retained
Backup power systems load-tested at least annually with results documenting capacity adequacy
HVAC systems maintained per manufacturer specifications with redundancy failover tested
commonGaps: Environmental system testing not conducted on schedule or test results not documented
Backup generator fuel supply not monitored or replenishment plan not defined
HVAC redundancy not tested under simulated failure conditions
suggestedSources: Fire suppression inspection and test certificates
Generator and UPS load test reports
HVAC maintenance and redundancy test records

evidenceItems.name: Third-Party Facility Environmental Requirements
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that environmental protection requirements are included in contracts with service providers who host organizational systems
whatGoodLooksLike: Service provider contracts specify environmental protection requirements and SLAs for uptime
